culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Jotham's parable of the trees is one of the oldest fables in world literature. Its political message -- that only worthless people seek power for its own sake -- has echoed through political philosophy for three millennia.

verses.text: “But the olive tree said to them, ‘Should I stop producing my oil, with which they honor God and man by me, and go to wave back and forth over the trees?’
verses.explainer: The olive tree refuses: 'Should I give up my oil, by which both gods and humans are honored, to hold sway over the trees?' Productive trees are too useful to waste their time ruling.

verses.text: “The trees said to the fig tree, ‘Come and reign over us.’
verses.explainer: The trees ask the fig tree to be their king. The fig tree also provides too much value to abandon its purpose for power.

verses.text: “But the fig tree said to them, ‘Should I leave my sweetness, and my good fruit, and go to wave back and forth over the trees?’
verses.explainer: The fig tree refuses: 'Should I give up my fruit, so good and sweet, to hold sway over the trees?' Again, genuine productivity declines the distraction of political power.

verses.text: “The trees said to the vine, ‘Come and reign over us.’
verses.explainer: The trees ask the vine to be their king. The vine, like the olive and fig, produces something of real value.

verses.text: “The vine said to them, ‘Should I leave my new wine, which cheers God and man, and go to wave back and forth over the trees?’
verses.explainer: The vine refuses: 'Should I give up my wine, which cheers both gods and humans, to hold sway over the trees?' The third refusal establishes the pattern -- worthy leaders don't seek power.

verses.text: “Then all the trees said to the bramble, ‘Come and reign over us.’
verses.explainer: Finally the trees ask the thornbush. The thornbush -- useless, parasitic, and dangerous -- is the only one willing to reign. Abimelech is the thornbush.

thenVsNow.then: Jotham's parable teaches that the most productive people rarely seek political power, while those who contribute least are most eager to rule. This ancient observation remains painfully relevant.
thenVsNow.now: Jotham's parable teaches that the most productive people rarely seek political power, while those who contribute least are most eager to rule. This ancient observation remains painfully relevant.

verses.text: “The bramble said to the trees, ‘If in truth you anoint me king over you, then come and take refuge in my shade; and if not, let fire come out of the bramble, and devour the cedars of Lebanon.’
verses.explainer: The thornbush responds: 'If you really want to anoint me king, come and take refuge in my shade. But if not, let fire come out of the thornbush and consume the cedars of Lebanon.' The thornbush threatens even the mightiest trees.

verses.text: “Now therefore, if you have dealt truly and righteously, in that you have made Abimelech king, and if you have dealt well with Jerubbaal and his house, and have done to him according to the deserving of his hands
verses.explainer: Jotham challenges: 'Did you act honorably when you made Abimelech king? Have you been faithful to Gideon's family?' The parable is now applied directly.

verses.text: (for my father fought for you, and risked his life, and delivered you out of the hand of Midian;
verses.explainer: Jotham reminds them that his father fought for them and risked his life to rescue them from Midian. Gideon's sacrifice deserves better than the murder of his sons.

verses.text: and you have risen up against my father’s house today, and have slain his sons, seventy persons, on one stone, and have made Abimelech, the son of his female servant, king over the men of Shechem, because he is your brother);
verses.explainer: But today you have risen up against my father's family, murdered seventy sons on a single stone, and made Abimelech king because he is your relative. The indictment is specific and devastating.

verses.text: if you then have dealt truly and righteously with Jerubbaal and with his house today, then rejoice in Abimelech, and let him also rejoice in you;
verses.explainer: If you have acted honorably toward Gideon's family, then may Abimelech be your joy and you his. But if not -- and the implication is clear that they have not --

verses.text: but if not, let fire come out from Abimelech, and devour the men of Shechem, and the house of Millo; and let fire come out from the men of Shechem, and from the house of Millo, and devour Abimelech.”
verses.explainer: Let fire come out from Abimelech and consume the citizens of Shechem, and let fire come out from the citizens of Shechem and consume Abimelech. Jotham prophesies mutual destruction.

verses.text: Jotham ran away, and fled, and went to Beer, and lived there, for fear of Abimelech his brother.
verses.explainer: Jotham flees to Beer and lives there because he is afraid of his brother Abimelech. The sole surviving son goes into exile.

verses.text: Abimelech was prince over Israel three years.
verses.explainer: After Abimelech governs Israel for three years, God sends an evil spirit between Abimelech and the citizens of Shechem. Divine judgment uses the instrument of internal discord.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The 'evil spirit' sent by God between Abimelech and Shechem operates through the natural dynamics of suspicion and betrayal between co-conspirators. God does not need supernatural intervention; the relationship between murderers naturally dissolves.

verses.text: Then God sent an evil spirit between Abimelech and the men of Shechem; and the men of Shechem dealt treacherously with Abimelech,
verses.explainer: The citizens of Shechem act treacherously against Abimelech. The alliance between the tyrant and his sponsors begins to fracture, exactly as Jotham prophesied.

verses.text: that the violence done to the seventy sons of Jerubbaal might come, and that their blood might be laid on Abimelech their brother, who killed them, and on the men of Shechem, who strengthened his hands to kill his brothers.
verses.explainer: God did this so that the crime against Gideon's seventy sons would be avenged on Abimelech and the citizens of Shechem who helped him murder his brothers. Divine justice operates through natural consequences.

verses.text: The men of Shechem set an ambush for him on the tops of the mountains, and they robbed all who came along that way by them, and Abimelech was told about it.
verses.explainer: The Shechemites set ambushes on the hilltops and rob everyone who passes by. Lawlessness replaces the order Abimelech promised to provide.

verses.text: Gaal the son of Ebed came with his brothers, and went over to Shechem; and the men of Shechem put their trust in him.
verses.explainer: Gaal son of Ebed moves to Shechem and gains the confidence of its citizens. A new rival emerges to challenge Abimelech's authority.

verses.text: They went out into the field, harvested their vineyards, trod the grapes, held festival, and went into the house of their god, and ate and drank, and cursed Abimelech.
verses.explainer: They go out to the vineyard, harvest grapes, hold a festival in the temple of their god, and curse Abimelech while eating and drinking. Religious festival becomes political rebellion.

verses.text: Gaal the son of Ebed said, “Who is Abimelech, and who is Shechem, that we should serve him? Isn’t he the son of Jerubbaal? Isn’t Zebul his officer? Serve the men of Hamor the father of Shechem, but why should we serve him?
verses.explainer: Gaal boasts: 'Who is Abimelech that we should serve him? Isn't he Gideon's son? Serve the men of Hamor, Shechem's father!' He appeals to pre-Israelite Shechemite identity.

verses.text: I wish that this people were under my hand! Then I would remove Abimelech.” He said to Abimelech, “Increase your army, and come out!”
verses.explainer: Gaal wishes he commanded Shechem's army -- he would remove Abimelech. 'Come out with your whole army!' he challenges. His bravado is fueled by wine and festival enthusiasm.

verses.text: When Zebul the ruler of the city heard the words of Gaal the son of Ebed, his anger burned.
verses.explainer: Zebul, the governor of the city and Abimelech's loyalist, hears Gaal's boasting and is furious. He secretly sends word to Abimelech about the rebellion.

verses.text: He sent messengers to Abimelech craftily, saying, “Behold, Gaal the son of Ebed and his brothers have come to Shechem; and behold, they incite the city against you.
verses.explainer: Zebul warns Abimelech that Gaal is stirring up the city and advises a night attack. The double-agent works against the populist rebel.

verses.text: Now therefore, go up by night, you and the people who are with you, and lie in wait in the field.
verses.explainer: Zebul tells Abimelech to set an ambush in the fields at night and attack at sunrise. The plan exploits the element of surprise.

verses.text: It shall be that in the morning, as soon as the sun is up, you shall rise early, and rush on the city. Behold, when he and the people who are with him come out against you, then may you do to them as you shall find occasion.”
verses.explainer: In the morning, Abimelech should attack the city. When Gaal comes out, Abimelech should do to him whatever he can. Zebul's plan is pragmatic and brutal.

verses.text: Abimelech rose up, and all the people who were with him, by night, and they laid wait against Shechem in four companies.
verses.explainer: Abimelech and his men set up ambush in four companies around Shechem during the night. The trap is laid.

verses.text: Gaal the son of Ebed went out, and stood in the entrance of the gate of the city. Abimelech rose up, and the people who were with him, from the ambush.
verses.explainer: When Gaal goes out and stands at the city gate, Abimelech and his men rise from ambush. The boastful rebel walks into a trap.

verses.text:
When Gaal saw the people, he said to Zebul, “Behold, people are coming down from the tops of the mountains.”
Zebul said to him, “You see the shadow of the mountains as if they were men.”
verses.explainer: Gaal sees the troops and tells Zebul: 'Look, people are coming down from the hilltops!' Zebul dismisses it: 'You're seeing shadows of the mountains.' Zebul buys time for the ambush to close.

verses.text: Gaal spoke again and said, “Behold, people are coming down by the middle of the land, and one company comes by the way of the oak of Meonenim.”
verses.explainer: Gaal insists: 'People are coming from the center of the land, and one company is coming from the direction of the Diviners' Oak.' The threat is real and closing in.

verses.text: Then Zebul said to him, “Now where is your mouth, that you said, ‘Who is Abimelech, that we should serve him?’ Isn’t this the people that you have despised? Please go out now and fight with them.”
verses.explainer: Zebul drops his pretense: 'Where is your big talk now? Isn't this the people you ridiculed? Go out and fight them!' The double-cross is complete.

verses.text: Gaal went out before the men of Shechem, and fought with Abimelech.
verses.explainer: Gaal leads the citizens of Shechem out and fights Abimelech. But Abimelech defeats him, and many fall wounded as Gaal flees to the gate.

verses.text: Abimelech chased him, and he fled before him, and many fell wounded, even to the entrance of the gate.
verses.explainer: Abimelech chases Gaal, who flees from Shechem. Zebul drives out Gaal and his clan, barring them from the city. The boastful rebel is expelled.

verses.text: Abimelech lived at Arumah; and Zebul drove out Gaal and his brothers, that they should not dwell in Shechem.
verses.explainer: Abimelech stays in Arumah, and Zebul drives out Gaal and his brothers so they cannot live in Shechem. The immediate rebellion is crushed.

verses.text: On the next day, the people went out into the field; and they told Abimelech.
verses.explainer: The next day, the people of Shechem go out to the fields. When Abimelech is told, he prepares for final vengeance against the city that dared to rebel.

verses.text: He took the people, and divided them into three companies, and laid wait in the field; and he looked, and behold, the people came out of the city. So, he rose up against them, and struck them.
verses.explainer: Abimelech divides his men into three companies, sets an ambush, and waits. When the people come out, he attacks them.

verses.text: Abimelech and the companies that were with him rushed forward, and stood in the entrance of the gate of the city; and the two companies rushed on all who were in the field, and struck them.
verses.explainer: Abimelech and his company rush to the city gate while two companies attack those in the fields. The coordinated assault cuts off escape routes.

verses.text: Abimelech fought against the city all that day; and he took the city, and killed the people in it. He beat down the city, and sowed it with salt.
verses.explainer: Abimelech fights all day, captures the city, kills its people, demolishes it, and scatters salt over it. Salting a city symbolizes its permanent desolation.

verses.text: When all the men of the tower of Shechem heard of it, they entered into the stronghold of the house of Elberith.
verses.explainer: When the citizens of the Tower of Shechem hear what has happened, they take refuge in the stronghold of the temple of El-berith. The temple fortress becomes their last stand.

verses.text: Abimelech was told that all the men of the tower of Shechem were gathered together.
verses.explainer: Abimelech learns that all the citizens of the Tower of Shechem have gathered in the temple stronghold. He plans their destruction.

verses.text: Abimelech went up to Mount Zalmon, he and all the people who were with him; and Abimelech took an ax in his hand, and cut down a bough from the trees, and took it up, and laid it on his shoulder. Then he said to the people who were with him, “What you have seen me do, make haste, and do as I have done!”
verses.explainer: Abimelech goes to Mount Zalmon, cuts a branch with an axe, puts it on his shoulder, and tells his men to do the same. Quick! Do what I do! Leadership by example, turned to destruction.

verses.text: All the people likewise each cut down his bough, and followed Abimelech, and put them at the base of the stronghold, and set the stronghold on fire on them; so that all the people of the tower of Shechem died also, about a thousand men and women.
verses.explainer: All the men cut branches and follow Abimelech. They pile them against the stronghold and set it on fire. About a thousand men and women die in the burning temple. Jotham's prophecy of fire is literally fulfilled.

verses.text: Then Abimelech went to Thebez, and encamped against Thebez, and took it.
verses.explainer: Abimelech then goes to Thebez and besieges it. His appetite for destruction drives him from city to city.

verses.text: But there was a strong tower within the city, and all the men and women of the city fled there, and shut themselves in, and went up to the roof of the tower.
verses.explainer: Inside Thebez is a strong tower. All the people flee to it, go up on the roof, and lock themselves in. The same pattern repeats -- people flee to a fortified tower.

verses.text: Abimelech came to the tower, and fought against it, and came near to the door of the tower to burn it with fire.
verses.explainer: Abimelech approaches the tower to set it on fire, just as he did at Shechem. His strategy is unchanged -- burn them alive.

verses.text: A certain woman cast an upper millstone on Abimelech’s head, and broke his skull.
verses.explainer: But a woman drops an upper millstone on Abimelech's head and cracks his skull. The mighty warrior who murdered seventy brothers is felled by a woman with a grinding stone.

crossReferences.text: Joab would later reference this event, showing that Abimelech's death by a woman's hand became a well-known cautionary tale.

verses.text: Then he called hastily to the young man his armor bearer, and said to him, “Draw your sword, and kill me, that men not say of me, ‘A woman killed him.’ His young man thrust him through, and he died.”
verses.explainer: Abimelech quickly calls his armor-bearer: 'Draw your sword and kill me, so that they can't say a woman killed me.' His final concern is not sin, not God, but reputation.

thenVsNow.then: Abimelech's dying concern is his reputation, not his soul. 'Don't let them say a woman killed me' reveals a value system where appearance matters more than reality, even at the moment of death.
thenVsNow.now: Abimelech's dying concern is his reputation, not his soul. 'Don't let them say a woman killed me' reveals a value system where appearance matters more than reality, even at the moment of death.

verses.text: When the men of Israel saw that Abimelech was dead, they each departed to his place.
verses.explainer: His armor-bearer runs him through and Abimelech dies. When his men see he is dead, they go home. The mercenary army dissolves when its paymaster dies.

verses.text: Thus God repaid the wickedness of Abimelech, which he did to his father, in killing his seventy brothers;
verses.explainer: Thus God repaid the wickedness that Abimelech had done to his father by murdering his seventy brothers. Divine justice is slow but certain.

verses.text: and God repaid all the wickedness of the men of Shechem on their heads; and the curse of Jotham the son of Jerubbaal came on them.
verses.explainer: God also made the people of Shechem pay for their wickedness. The curse of Jotham son of Gideon came on them. Jotham's prophecy of mutual destruction is completely fulfilled.

explainer.summary: Chapter 10 covers two minor judges (Tola and Jair) and the escalating Ammonite crisis. When Israel cries out, God initially refuses to help, delivering the sharpest divine rebuke in the book: 'Go cry to the gods you have chosen.' Israel's unconditional repentance moves God to compassion, setting the stage for Jephthah.
themes: God's patience has limits
Genuine repentance vs. crisis-driven prayer
The expanding catalogue of Israel's idolatry
God's compassion overcoming righteous anger
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 10's divine dialogue is the theological center of the book. God's refusal-then-relenting demonstrates the tension between justice and mercy that characterizes the entire judges period.

wordStudies.translatedAs: God 'could bear Israel's misery no longer' -- the
wordStudies.meaning: God 'could bear Israel's misery no longer' -- the Hebrew suggests God's patience was shortened or his soul was vexed. Even divine patience has a breaking point, and divine compassion has a limit beyond which it must act.
wordStudies.significance: God 'could bear Israel's misery no longer' -- the Hebrew suggests God's patience was shortened or hi

wordStudies.translatedAs: To abandon, leave, or desert
wordStudies.meaning: To abandon, leave, or desert. Israel forsook God to serve other gods; God threatens to forsake Israel to their consequences. The same verb used for both sides emphasizes the symmetry of covenant relationship.
wordStudies.significance: To abandon, leave, or desert. Israel forsook God to serve other gods; God threatens to forsake Israe

verses.text: After Abimelech, Tola the son of Puah, the son of Dodo, a man of Issachar, arose to save Israel. He lived in Shamir in the hill country of Ephraim.
verses.explainer: After Abimelech, Tola son of Puah, from the tribe of Issachar, rises to save Israel. He lives at Shamir in the hill country of Ephraim. Tola is a 'minor judge' whose story is compressed.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Minor judges like Tola and Jair may have been regional administrators rather than military deliverers. Their long, peaceful reigns suggest that stable governance, not just military victory, was part of God's provision for Israel.

verses.text: He judged Israel twenty-three years, and died, and was buried in Shamir.
verses.explainer: Tola leads Israel twenty-three years, then dies and is buried at Shamir. No military exploits are recorded -- perhaps his greatest achievement was stability after Abimelech's chaos.

verses.text: After him Jair, the Gileadite arose, and he judged Israel twenty-two years.
verses.explainer: He is followed by Jair of Gilead, who leads Israel twenty-two years. Two decades of quiet leadership from the Transjordan region.

verses.text: He had thirty sons who rode on thirty donkey colts, and they had thirty cities, which are called Havvoth Jair to this day, which are in the land of Gilead.
verses.explainer: Jair has thirty sons who ride thirty donkeys and control thirty towns in Gilead, called Havvoth Jair ('settlements of Jair'). His wealth and influence are extensive.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Donkey-riding sons indicate wealth and status. Donkeys were the prestigious transportation of the pre-monarchic period; horses were not common in Israel until Solomon's time.

verses.text: Jair died, and was buried in Kamon.
verses.explainer: Jair dies and is buried in Kamon. Two minor judges, forty-five combined years, provide a transition between the Gideon-Abimelech crisis and the Jephthah narrative.

verses.text: The children of Israel again did that which was evil in Yahweh’s sight, and served the Baals, the Ashtaroth, the gods of Syria, the gods of Sidon, the gods of Moab, the gods of the children of Ammon, and the gods of the Philistines. They abandoned Yahweh, and didn’t serve him.
verses.explainer: Again the Israelites do evil, serving the Baals, Ashtoreths, the gods of Aram, Sidon, Moab, Ammon, and the Philistines. The list of foreign gods grows longer -- apostasy is widening.

crossReferences.text: The cycle described in chapter 2 plays out again, but this time with divine resistance added to the pattern.

verses.text: Yahweh’s anger burned against Israel, and he sold them into the hand of the Philistines, and into the hand of the children of Ammon.
verses.explainer: The LORD's anger burns against Israel and he sells them into the hands of the Philistines and Ammonites. Two oppressors simultaneously, from west and east.

verses.text: They troubled and oppressed the children of Israel that year. For eighteen years, they oppressed all the children of Israel that were beyond the Jordan in the land of the Amorites, which is in Gilead.
verses.explainer: For eighteen years, the Ammonites shatter and crush the Israelites east of the Jordan in Gilead. The Transjordan tribes bear the brunt of Ammonite aggression.

verses.text: The children of Ammon passed over the Jordan to fight also against Judah, and against Benjamin, and against the house of Ephraim, so that Israel was very distressed.
verses.explainer: The Ammonites also cross the Jordan to fight Judah, Benjamin, and Ephraim. Israel is in great distress as the eastern enemy invades the western heartland.

verses.text: The children of Israel cried to Yahweh, saying, “We have sinned against you, even because we have forsaken our God, and have served the Baals.”
verses.explainer: The Israelites cry out to the LORD: 'We have sinned against you, forsaking our God and serving the Baals.' For the first time, the cry includes a specific confession of sin.

verses.text: Yahweh said to the children of Israel, “Didn’t I save you from the Egyptians, and from the Amorites, from the children of Ammon, and from the Philistines?
verses.explainer: The LORD answers with a list of past deliverances: from Egypt, the Amorites, the Ammonites, the Philistines. God rehearses his track record of faithfulness.

verses.text: The Sidonians also, and the Amalekites, and the Maonites, oppressed you; and you cried to me, and I saved you out of their hand.
verses.explainer: From the Sidonians, Amalekites, and Maonites -- when you cried out to me, I saved you. Seven past deliverances are named.

verses.text: Yet you have forsaken me, and served other gods. Therefore I will save you no more.
verses.explainer: But you have forsaken me and served other gods, so I will no longer save you. God's patience has limits. This is the most direct divine refusal in the book.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: God's refusal to deliver ('I will no longer save you') is unprecedented in Judges. Every previous cry was immediately answered. This refusal marks a turning point -- God demands genuine repentance, not just desperate prayer.

crossReferences.text: Isaiah would later echo God's weariness with insincere worship: 'I have more than enough of burnt offerings.'

verses.text: Go and cry to the gods which you have chosen. Let them save you in the time of your distress!”
verses.explainer: Go and cry out to the gods you have chosen. Let them save you when you are in trouble. God's challenge exposes the impotence of Israel's adopted gods.

thenVsNow.then: God's challenge -- 'let the gods you chose save you' -- is not cruelty but exposure therapy. Only by discovering that false gods cannot deliver can Israel genuinely return to the true God.
thenVsNow.now: God's challenge -- 'let the gods you chose save you' -- is not cruelty but exposure therapy. Only by discovering that false gods cannot deliver can Israel genuinely return to the true God.

verses.text: The children of Israel said to Yahweh, “We have sinned! Do you to us whatever seems good to you; only deliver us, please, today.”
verses.explainer: The Israelites respond: 'We have sinned. Do with us whatever you think best, but please rescue us now.' Their surrender is unconditional -- 'do whatever you want, just save us.'

verses.text: They put away the foreign gods from among them, and served Yahweh; and his soul was grieved for the misery of Israel.
verses.explainer: They get rid of the foreign gods and serve the LORD. And he can bear Israel's misery no longer. God's compassion overcomes his judgment -- he cannot watch them suffer.

thenVsNow.then: God 'could bear Israel's misery no longer' reveals something profound about divine character: God's compassion ultimately overrules his judgment. His mercy is stronger than his anger.
thenVsNow.now: God 'could bear Israel's misery no longer' reveals something profound about divine character: God's compassion ultimately overrules his judgment. His mercy is stronger than his anger.

verses.text: Then the children of Ammon were gathered together and encamped in Gilead. The children of Israel assembled themselves together and encamped in Mizpah.
verses.explainer: When the Ammonites are called to arms and camp in Gilead, the Israelites assemble and camp at Mizpah. Battle lines form in the Transjordan.

verses.text: The people, the princes of Gilead, said to one another, “Who is the man who will begin to fight against the children of Ammon? He shall be head over all the inhabitants of Gilead.”
verses.explainer: The leaders of Gilead ask each other: 'Who will be the first to attack the Ammonites? Whoever he is, he will be head over all the people of Gilead.' They seek a leader -- setting up Jephthah's entrance in chapter 11.

explainer.summary: Chapter 11 tells Jephthah's story: rejected outcast turned military leader, skilled diplomat who argues Israel's case against Ammon with historical precision, Spirit-empowered warrior who wins decisively, and tragic father who fulfills a rash vow at the cost of his daughter. It is one of the most theologically complex and emotionally devastating chapters in the Bible.
themes: The outcast chosen as deliverer
Diplomacy before warfare
The devastating consequences of rash vows
Faith mixed with theological confusion
explainer.contextInBook: Jephthah represents a further decline from the Gideon era. He knows enough about God to invoke Him but not enough to know that God does not require human sacrifice. His story embodies the theological confusion of the judges period.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A solemn promise to God, considered absolutely bin
wordStudies.meaning: A solemn promise to God, considered absolutely binding in ancient Israel (Numbers 30:2). Jephthah's vow follows ancient Near Eastern patterns where warriors promised offerings in exchange for victory. However, Israelite law prohibited human sacrifice.
wordStudies.significance: A solemn promise to God, considered absolutely binding in ancient Israel (Numbers 30:2). Jephthah's

wordStudies.translatedAs: A sacrifice entirely consumed by fire on the altar
wordStudies.meaning: A sacrifice entirely consumed by fire on the altar, symbolizing total dedication to God. Jephthah's promise to offer 'whatever comes out of my house' as a burnt offering implies he either expected an animal or was recklessly willing to sacrifice a person.
wordStudies.significance: A sacrifice entirely consumed by fire on the altar, symbolizing total dedication to God. Jephthah's

verses.text: Now Jephthah the Gileadite was a mighty man of valor, and he was the son of a prostitute. Gilead became the father of Jephthah.
verses.explainer: Jephthah the Gileadite is a mighty warrior but also the son of a prostitute. His father is Gilead. His origin ensures social marginalization despite his military talent.

verses.text: Gilead’s wife bore him sons. When his wife’s sons grew up, they drove Jephthah out, and said to him, “You will not inherit in our father’s house, for you are the son of another woman.”
verses.explainer: Gilead's legitimate wife bears sons who, when grown, drive Jephthah away: 'You are not going to get any inheritance in our family, because you are the son of another woman.' Rejected by his own brothers.

verses.text: Then Jephthah fled from his brothers, and lived in the land of Tob. Outlaws joined up with Jephthah, and they went out with him.
verses.explainer: Jephthah flees to the land of Tob, where a gang of scoundrels gathers around him. Like David after him, the outcast becomes the leader of other outcasts.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Outcasts who became military leaders were common in the ancient Near East. David's gathering of outcasts at Adullam Cave (1 Samuel 22:2) closely parallels Jephthah's experience.

verses.text: After a while, the children of Ammon made war against Israel.
verses.explainer: Some time later, the Ammonites make war on Israel. The crisis that created the outcast now requires the outcast's skills.

verses.text: When the children of Ammon made war against Israel, the elders of Gilead went to get Jephthah out of the land of Tob.
verses.explainer: When the Ammonites attack, the elders of Gilead go to get Jephthah from Tob. The same community that expelled him now needs him desperately.

verses.text: They said to Jephthah, “Come and be our chief, that we may fight with the children of Ammon.”
verses.explainer: They ask Jephthah to be their commander and lead the fight against Ammon. The transition from outcast to commander is swift and ironic.

verses.text: Jephthah said to the elders of Gilead, “Didn’t you hate me, and drive me out of my father’s house? Why have you come to me now when you are in distress?”
verses.explainer: Jephthah confronts them: 'Didn't you hate me and drive me from my father's house? Why come to me now that you're in trouble?' His grievance is legitimate and his question is fair.

thenVsNow.then: Jephthah's rejection by his family and later need by his community is a pattern familiar to anyone who has been marginalized until their skills are suddenly needed. The community's about-face reveals that values often yield to necessity.
thenVsNow.now: Jephthah's rejection by his family and later need by his community is a pattern familiar to anyone who has been marginalized until their skills are suddenly needed. The community's about-face reveals that values often yield to necessity.

verses.text: The elders of Gilead said to Jephthah, “Therefore we have turned again to you now, that you may go with us, and fight with the children of Ammon. You will be our head over all the inhabitants of Gilead.”
verses.explainer: The elders promise: 'If you come back and fight the Ammonites, you will be our head.' They offer permanent leadership, not just temporary military command.

verses.text: Jephthah said to the elders of Gilead, “If you bring me home again to fight with the children of Ammon, and Yahweh delivers them before me, will I be your head?”
verses.explainer: Jephthah asks for clarity: 'If I fight the Ammonites and the LORD gives them to me, will I really be your head?' He wants the promise to be explicit and binding.

verses.text: The elders of Gilead said to Jephthah, “Yahweh will be witness between us. Surely we will do what you say.”
verses.explainer: The elders swear: 'The LORD is our witness; we will certainly do as you say.' The agreement is sealed with a divine oath.

verses.text: Then Jephthah went with the elders of Gilead, and the people made him head and chief over them. Jephthah spoke all his words before Yahweh in Mizpah.
verses.explainer: Jephthah goes with the elders and the people make him head and commander. He repeats all his terms before the LORD at Mizpah, making the covenant public and sacred.

verses.text: Jephthah sent messengers to the king of the children of Ammon, saying, “What have you to do with me, that you have come to me to fight against my land?”
verses.explainer: Jephthah sends messengers to the Ammonite king asking: 'What do you have against us that you have attacked our country?' He attempts diplomacy before warfare.

verses.text: The king of the children of Ammon answered to the messengers of Jephthah, “Because Israel took away my land, when he came up out of Egypt, from the Arnon even to the Jabbok, and to the Jordan. Now therefore restore that territory again peaceably.”
verses.explainer: The Ammonite king claims Israel took his land from the Arnon to the Jabbok to the Jordan when they came up from Egypt. He demands its return. The territorial dispute has historical roots.

verses.text: Jephthah sent messengers again to the king of the children of Ammon;
verses.explainer: Jephthah sends messengers again with a detailed historical argument refuting the Ammonite claim. He proves to be not just a warrior but a skilled diplomat and historian.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Jephthah's historical argument (vv. 15-27) demonstrates sophisticated knowledge of Israel's history and international law. His diplomatic skill contrasts sharply with the rash vow that follows.

verses.text: and he said to him, “Thus says Jephthah: Israel didn’t take away the land of Moab, nor the land of the children of Ammon,
verses.explainer: Jephthah argues that Israel did not take the land of Moab or Ammon. The historical review begins with Israel's journey from Egypt.

verses.text: but when they came up from Egypt, and Israel went through the wilderness to the Red Sea, and came to Kadesh;
verses.explainer: When Israel came from Egypt and went through the wilderness to the Red Sea and then to Kadesh, they respected national boundaries.

verses.text: then Israel sent messengers to the king of Edom, saying, ‘Please let me pass through your land;’ but the king of Edom didn’t listen. In the same way, he sent to the king of Moab; but he would not: and Israel stayed in Kadesh.
verses.explainer: Israel sent messengers to the king of Edom asking for permission to pass through, but was refused. They also asked Moab, who also refused. Israel stayed at Kadesh rather than force passage.

verses.text: Then they went through the wilderness, and went around the land of Edom, and the land of Moab, and came by the east side of the land of Moab, and they encamped on the other side of the Arnon; but they didn’t come within the border of Moab, for the Arnon was the border of Moab.
verses.explainer: Israel went around Edom and Moab, traveling along the eastern side, and camped along the Arnon without entering Moabite territory. They respected borders.

verses.text: Israel sent messengers to Sihon king of the Amorites, the king of Heshbon; and Israel said to him, ‘Please let us pass through your land to my place.’
verses.explainer: Israel sent messengers to Sihon king of the Amorites at Heshbon, asking for peaceful passage. Diplomacy was attempted first.

verses.text: But Sihon didn’t trust Israel to pass through his border; but Sihon gathered all his people together, and encamped in Jahaz, and fought against Israel.
verses.explainer: Sihon refused and attacked Israel at Jahaz. The Amorites, not Israel, initiated the warfare.

verses.text: Yahweh, the God of Israel, delivered Sihon and all his people into the hand of Israel, and they struck them. So Israel possessed all the land of the Amorites, the inhabitants of that country.
verses.explainer: The LORD gave Sihon and his army to Israel. Israel took possession of all the land of the Amorites in that region. The land was taken from the Amorites, not the Ammonites.

crossReferences.text: The historical events Jephthah references are recorded in Numbers, confirming his accuracy.

verses.text: They possessed all the border of the Amorites, from the Arnon even to the Jabbok, and from the wilderness even to the Jordan.
verses.explainer: Israel conquered all Amorite territory from the Arnon to the Jabbok, from the desert to the Jordan. This was Amorite land, not Ammonite land.

verses.text: So now Yahweh, the God of Israel, has dispossessed the Amorites from before his people Israel, and should you possess them?
verses.explainer: Jephthah's conclusion: 'The LORD, the God of Israel, has driven out the Amorites before his people Israel. Should you then possess it? Your god Chemosh gives you what he gives you, and the LORD gives us what he gives us.'

verses.text: Won’t you possess that which Chemosh your god gives you to possess? So whoever Yahweh our God has dispossessed from before us, them will we possess.
verses.explainer: Jephthah argues by analogy: whatever Chemosh your god gives you, keep. Whatever the LORD our God gives us, we will possess. He uses their own theological framework against them.

verses.text: Now are you anything better than Balak the son of Zippor, king of Moab? Did he ever strive against Israel, or did he ever fight against them?
verses.explainer: Are you any better than Balak son of Zippor, king of Moab? Did he ever quarrel with Israel or fight against them? Even Moab's king accepted the situation.

verses.text: While Israel lived in Heshbon and its towns, and in Aroer and its towns, and in all the cities that are along by the side of the Arnon, three hundred years; why didn’t you recover them within that time?
verses.explainer: Israel has occupied these towns for three hundred years. Why didn't Ammon reclaim them during that time? The passage of time validates possession.

verses.text: I therefore have not sinned against you, but you do me wrong to war against me. May Yahweh the Judge be judge today between the children of Israel and the children of Ammon.”
verses.explainer: Jephthah declares: 'I have not wronged you, but you are doing me wrong by waging war against me. Let the LORD, the Judge, decide the dispute today.' He submits the case to divine arbitration.

verses.text: However the king of the children of Ammon didn’t listen to the words of Jephthah which he sent him.
verses.explainer: The king of the Ammonites pays no attention to Jephthah's message. Diplomacy fails; war becomes inevitable.

verses.text: Then Yahweh’s Spirit came on Jephthah, and he passed over Gilead and Manasseh, and passed over Mizpah of Gilead, and from Mizpah of Gilead he passed over to the children of Ammon.
verses.explainer: The Spirit of the LORD comes on Jephthah. He crosses Gilead and Manasseh, passes through Mizpah of Gilead, and advances against the Ammonites.

verses.text: Jephthah vowed a vow to Yahweh, and said, “If you will indeed deliver the children of Ammon into my hand,
verses.explainer: Jephthah makes a vow to the LORD: 'If you give the Ammonites into my hands, whatever comes out of the door of my house to meet me when I return will be the LORD's, and I will sacrifice it as a burnt offering.'

crossReferences.text: God explicitly prohibited child sacrifice: 'Do not give any of your children to be sacrificed to Molek.'

thenVsNow.then: Jephthah's rash vow reveals the danger of treating God as a vending machine: insert a promise, receive a blessing. God does not need to be bribed to act, and promises that violate His character should never be made.
thenVsNow.now: Jephthah's rash vow reveals the danger of treating God as a vending machine: insert a promise, receive a blessing. God does not need to be bribed to act, and promises that violate His character should never be made.

verses.text: then it shall be, that whatever comes out of the doors of my house to meet me, when I return in peace from the children of Ammon, it shall be Yahweh’s, and I will offer it up for a burnt offering.”
verses.explainer: The vow is made. Its consequences will prove devastating. Jephthah, the warrior-diplomat, makes a rash promise that his victory will force him to fulfill.

verses.text: So Jephthah passed over to the children of Ammon to fight against them; and Yahweh delivered them into his hand.
verses.explainer: Jephthah crosses over to fight the Ammonites, and the LORD gives them into his hands. The military victory is decisive.

verses.text: He struck them from Aroer until you come to Minnith, even twenty cities, and to Abelcheramim, with a very great slaughter. So the children of Ammon were subdued before the children of Israel.
verses.explainer: He devastates twenty towns from Aroer to Minnith and Abel-keramim. The Ammonites are subdued before the Israelites. The victory is comprehensive.

verses.text: Jephthah came to Mizpah to his house; and behold, his daughter came out to meet him with tambourines and with dances. She was his only child. Besides her he had neither son nor daughter.
verses.explainer: When Jephthah returns home, his daughter comes out to meet him, dancing with tambourines. She is his only child -- he has no other son or daughter.

verses.text: When he saw her, he tore his clothes, and said, “Alas, my daughter! You have brought me very low, and you are one of those who trouble me; for I have opened my mouth to Yahweh, and I can’t go back.”
verses.explainer: When he sees her, he tears his clothes and cries: 'Oh, my daughter! You have brought me very low! You are among those who trouble me! I have made a vow to the LORD that I cannot break.'

verses.text: She said to him, “My father, you have opened your mouth to Yahweh; do to me according to that which has proceeded out of your mouth, because Yahweh has taken vengeance for you on your enemies, even on the children of Ammon.”
verses.explainer: His daughter responds with remarkable faith and acceptance: 'My father, you have given your word to the LORD. Do to me just as you promised, now that the LORD has avenged you of your enemies.'

thenVsNow.then: The daughter's acceptance of her father's vow with grace and faith makes the tragedy even deeper. Her courage surpasses her father's -- she faces the consequences of his recklessness with dignity he does not deserve.
thenVsNow.now: The daughter's acceptance of her father's vow with grace and faith makes the tragedy even deeper. Her courage surpasses her father's -- she faces the consequences of his recklessness with dignity he does not deserve.

verses.text: She said to her father, “Let this thing be done for me. Leave me alone two months, that I may depart and go down on the mountains, and bewail my virginity, I and my companions.”
verses.explainer: She asks only for two months to roam the hills with her friends and weep because she will never marry. The tragedy is compounded by the loss of potential descendants.

verses.text: He said, “Go.” He sent her away for two months: and she departed, she and her companions, and mourned her virginity on the mountains.
verses.explainer: He lets her go for two months. She and her friends weep in the hills over her virginity -- not her death, but her childlessness and unfulfilled life.

verses.text: At the end of two months, she returned to her father, who did with her according to his vow which he had vowed. She was a virgin. It was a custom in Israel
verses.explainer: After two months she returns, and he does to her as he had vowed. She had never known a man. The narrator's restraint makes the tragedy more powerful -- no details, just the devastating fact.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The debate about whether Jephthah actually sacrificed his daughter or dedicated her to lifelong virginity has divided interpreters for millennia. The text's language strongly suggests an actual sacrifice, which the narrator presents as tragic, not exemplary.

verses.text: that the daughters of Israel went yearly to celebrate the daughter of Jephthah the Gileadite four days in a year.
verses.explainer: This becomes an Israelite tradition: annually, the young women of Israel go out for four days to commemorate the daughter of Jephthah. Her name is never recorded, but her memory is preserved.

thenVsNow.then: That Jephthah's daughter is never named while her memory is annually commemorated creates a painful paradox. She is remembered but unnamed -- honored but erased. The text itself seems to grieve this injustice.
thenVsNow.now: That Jephthah's daughter is never named while her memory is annually commemorated creates a painful paradox. She is remembered but unnamed -- honored but erased. The text itself seems to grieve this injustice.

explainer.summary: Chapter 12 opens with another Ephraimite confrontation, but unlike Gideon's diplomatic resolution, Jephthah's response leads to civil war. The 'Shibboleth' test -- using dialect differences to identify and kill Ephraimite fugitives -- results in 42,000 deaths. The chapter concludes with three minor judges: Ibzan, Elon, and Abdon.
themes: Escalation from diplomacy to civil war
The failure of tribal unity
Dialect and identity as matters of life and death
The contrast between minor judges' stability and major judges' drama
explainer.contextInBook: The Shibboleth incident represents a new low: Israelites killing Israelites on a massive scale. The civil war that Gideon's diplomacy prevented in chapter 8 erupts under Jephthah, showing the progressive deterioration of leadership and community.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Shibboleth means 'ear of grain' or 'stream
wordStudies.meaning: Shibboleth means 'ear of grain' or 'stream.' The Ephraimite dialect could not produce the 'sh' sound, saying 'Sibboleth' instead. This is the origin of the English word 'shibboleth,' meaning a linguistic or cultural test used to identify outsiders.
wordStudies.significance: Shibboleth means 'ear of grain' or 'stream.' The Ephraimite dialect could not produce the 'sh' sound

wordStudies.translatedAs: A shallow crossing point in a river
wordStudies.meaning: A shallow crossing point in a river. Controlling the Jordan fords was a decisive military tactic, trapping enemies between the river and the pursuing army.
wordStudies.significance: A shallow crossing point in a river. Controlling the Jordan fords was a decisive military tactic, tr

verses.text: The men of Ephraim were gathered together, and passed northward; and they said to Jephthah, “Why did you pass over to fight against the children of Ammon, and didn’t call us to go with you? We will burn your house around you with fire!”
verses.explainer: The Ephraimites mobilize and cross to Zaphon, confronting Jephthah: 'Why did you go to fight the Ammonites without calling us? We're going to burn down your house over your head!' The same complaint they raised against Gideon, now with a death threat.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The Ephraimites' complaint echoes Judges 8:1, where they confronted Gideon. Ephraim's tribal pride and insistence on being included in military campaigns was a persistent source of inter-tribal conflict.

crossReferences.text: Gideon faced the same Ephraimite complaint but resolved it diplomatically. Jephthah's harsher response shows declining leadership quality.

thenVsNow.then: The contrast between Gideon's diplomatic handling of Ephraim and Jephthah's military response illustrates how similar situations can escalate or de-escalate depending on leadership. Words can prevent wars that weapons cannot.
thenVsNow.now: The contrast between Gideon's diplomatic handling of Ephraim and Jephthah's military response illustrates how similar situations can escalate or de-escalate depending on leadership. Words can prevent wars that weapons cannot.

verses.text: Jephthah said to them, “I and my people were at great strife with the children of Ammon; and when I called you, you didn’t save me out of their hand.
verses.explainer: Jephthah explains that he and his people were engaged in a great struggle with the Ammonites, and when he called for help, Ephraim did not come. His defense is that he did call them.

verses.text: When I saw that you didn’t save me, I put my life in my hand, and passed over against the children of Ammon, and Yahweh delivered them into my hand. Why then have you come up to me today, to fight against me?”
verses.explainer: When Jephthah saw that Ephraim would not help, he risked his own life and crossed over to fight the Ammonites, and the LORD gave him the victory. The blame lies with Ephraim's inaction, not Jephthah's exclusion.

verses.text: Then Jephthah gathered together all the men of Gilead, and fought with Ephraim. The men of Gilead struck Ephraim, because they said, “You are fugitives of Ephraim, you Gileadites, in the middle of Ephraim, and in the middle of Manasseh.”
verses.explainer: Jephthah calls together the men of Gilead and fights Ephraim. Gilead attacks because the Ephraimites had taunted them: 'You Gileadites are renegades from Ephraim and Manasseh.' The insult triggers civil war.

verses.text: The Gileadites took the fords of the Jordan against the Ephraimites. When the fugitives of Ephraim said, “Let me go over,” the men of Gilead said to him, “Are you an Ephraimite?” If he said, “No”;
verses.explainer: The Gileadites capture the fords of the Jordan against the Ephraimites. Anyone trying to cross is interrogated. Control of river crossings determines who escapes.

verses.text: then they said to him, “Now say ‘Shibboleth;’” and he said “Sibboleth”; for he couldn’t manage to pronounce it right: then they seized him, and killed him at the fords of the Jordan. At that time, forty-two thousand of Ephraim fell.
verses.explainer: When an Ephraimite fugitive asks to cross, they say: 'Say Shibboleth.' He says 'Sibboleth' because he cannot pronounce it correctly. They seize him and kill him. Forty-two thousand Ephraimites fall.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The scale of the Shibboleth slaughter -- 42,000 -- exceeds the casualties in many of Israel's battles against foreign enemies. The civil war was more destructive than foreign oppression.

thenVsNow.then: The 'Shibboleth' test has given us a word for any test that identifies insiders and outsiders. Language, accent, and cultural markers still serve as boundaries -- sometimes benign, sometimes deadly.
thenVsNow.now: The 'Shibboleth' test has given us a word for any test that identifies insiders and outsiders. Language, accent, and cultural markers still serve as boundaries -- sometimes benign, sometimes deadly.

verses.text: Jephthah judged Israel six years. Then Jephthah the Gileadite died, and was buried in the cities of Gilead.
verses.explainer: Jephthah leads Israel for six years and then dies. He is buried in a town in Gilead. His judgeship is brief compared to the long peace other judges achieved.

verses.text: After him Ibzan of Bethlehem judged Israel.
verses.explainer: After Jephthah, Ibzan of Bethlehem leads Israel. Another minor judge whose story is compressed into a few verses.

verses.text: He had thirty sons; and thirty daughters he sent outside his clan, and thirty daughters he brought in from outside his clan for his sons. He judged Israel seven years.
verses.explainer: Ibzan has thirty sons and thirty daughters. He sends his daughters abroad in marriage and brings in thirty young women for his sons. His extensive marriage alliances indicate wealth and political influence.

verses.text: Ibzan died, and was buried at Bethlehem.
verses.explainer: Ibzan leads Israel seven years, then dies and is buried at Bethlehem. A brief, apparently peaceful tenure.

verses.text: After him Elon the Zebulunite judged Israel; and he judged Israel ten years.
verses.explainer: After Ibzan, Elon the Zebulunite leads Israel. His story is even more compressed.

verses.text: Elon the Zebulunite died, and was buried in Aijalon in the land of Zebulun.
verses.explainer: Elon leads Israel ten years, then dies and is buried at Aijalon in the territory of Zebulun. His ten years pass without recorded incident.

verses.text: After him Abdon the son of Hillel the Pirathonite judged Israel.
verses.explainer: After Elon, Abdon son of Hillel from Pirathon leads Israel. The last of the minor judges in this sequence.

verses.text: He had forty sons and thirty sons’ sons, who rode on seventy donkey colts. He judged Israel eight years.
verses.explainer: Abdon has forty sons and thirty grandsons who ride seventy donkeys. His large family and many donkeys indicate wealth, status, and effective governance.

verses.text: Abdon the son of Hillel the Pirathonite died, and was buried in Pirathon in the land of Ephraim, in the hill country of the Amalekites.
verses.explainer: Abdon leads Israel eight years, then dies and is buried at Pirathon in the hill country of the Amalekites, in Ephraim. The chapter closes with Israel heading toward its darkest period.

explainer.summary: Chapter 13 narrates the annunciation and birth of Samson, the last major judge. An angel appears to Manoah's barren wife, announcing that she will bear a Nazirite son who will 'begin' to deliver Israel from the Philistines. The chapter features a theophany and reveals the wife's superior theological insight compared to her husband's confusion.
themes: Divine calling from before birth
The Nazirite vow and total dedication to God
The contrast between the wife's faith and Manoah's anxiety
God's sovereignty over barrenness and birth
explainer.contextInBook: Samson's elaborate birth narrative -- the longest in Judges -- creates expectations of a great deliverer. The irony is devastating: this divinely announced, Spirit-empowered Nazirite will become the most morally compromised judge of all.

wordStudies.translatedAs: From nazar, 'to separate' or 'to consecrate
wordStudies.meaning: From nazar, 'to separate' or 'to consecrate.' A Nazirite was set apart for God through three restrictions: no grape products, no contact with the dead, and no cutting of hair. Samson's Nazirite status was lifelong, unlike the typical temporary vow.
wordStudies.significance: From nazar, 'to separate' or 'to consecrate.' A Nazirite was set apart for God through three restric

wordStudies.translatedAs: Related to the word pele, 'wonder' or 'miracle
wordStudies.meaning: Related to the word pele, 'wonder' or 'miracle.' The angel's name is 'beyond understanding' -- it belongs to the realm of the divine, not the human. This is the same word used in Isaiah 9:6 for the Messiah's name 'Wonderful.'
wordStudies.significance: Related to the word pele, 'wonder' or 'miracle.' The angel's name is 'beyond understanding' -- it be

verses.text: The children of Israel again did that which was evil in Yahweh’s sight; and Yahweh delivered them into the hand of the Philistines forty years.
verses.explainer: Again the Israelites do evil, and the LORD delivers them into the hands of the Philistines for forty years. This is the longest oppression in Judges and the last major cycle.

verses.text: There was a certain man of Zorah, of the family of the Danites, whose name was Manoah; and his wife was barren, and childless.
verses.explainer: A man from Zorah named Manoah, from the clan of Dan, has a wife who is barren. The barren-woman-bears-a-special-child motif signals a divinely appointed figure.

verses.text: Yahweh’s angel appeared to the woman, and said to her, “See now, you are barren and childless; but you shall conceive, and bear a son.
verses.explainer: The angel of the LORD appears to the woman: 'You are barren and childless, but you will conceive and bear a son.' An annunciation scene comparable to those of Isaac, Samuel, and John the Baptist.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The annunciation pattern -- angel appears to a woman, announces a special birth, gives specific instructions -- recurs throughout Scripture. Each such birth produces a pivotal figure in salvation history.

verses.text: Now therefore please beware and drink no wine nor strong drink, and don’t eat any unclean thing:
verses.explainer: She is instructed to drink no wine or fermented drink and eat nothing unclean. The Nazirite restrictions begin even before the child's birth, in the mother's womb.

verses.text: for, behold, you shall conceive, and give birth to a son. No razor shall come on his head; for the child shall be a Nazirite to God from the womb. He shall begin to save Israel out of the hand of the Philistines.”
verses.explainer: No razor shall touch his head because the boy will be a Nazirite, dedicated to God from the womb. He will begin the deliverance of Israel from the Philistines. Note: begin, not complete.

crossReferences.text: The Nazirite vow is detailed in Numbers. Samson's lifelong Nazirite status is unusual -- most Nazirite vows were temporary.

thenVsNow.then: Samson's calling begins before birth, demonstrating that God's purposes for people can precede their existence. The Nazirite vow from the womb means Samson was never his own -- he belonged to God from the start.
thenVsNow.now: Samson's calling begins before birth, demonstrating that God's purposes for people can precede their existence. The Nazirite vow from the womb means Samson was never his own -- he belonged to God from the start.

verses.text: Then the woman came and told her husband, saying, “A man of God came to me, and his face was like the face of the angel of God, very awesome. I didn’t ask him where he was from, neither did he tell me his name;
verses.explainer: The woman tells her husband: 'A man of God came to me. He looked like an angel of God, very awesome. I didn't ask where he came from, and he didn't tell me his name.'

verses.text: but he said to me, ‘Behold, you shall conceive, and bear a son; and now drink no wine nor strong drink. Don’t eat any unclean thing; for the child shall be a Nazirite to God from the womb to the day of his death.’”
verses.explainer: She reports: he said I will conceive a son, and I must drink no wine or eat anything unclean because the boy will be a Nazirite from the womb to the day of his death.

verses.text: Then Manoah entreated Yahweh, and said, “Oh, Lord, please let the man of God whom you sent come again to us, and teach us what we should do to the child who shall be born.”
verses.explainer: Manoah prays: 'Lord, please let the man of God come again to teach us how to bring up the boy.' A father's earnest prayer for parenting wisdom.

verses.text: God listened to the voice of Manoah, and the angel of God came again to the woman as she sat in the field, but Manoah, her husband, wasn’t with her.
verses.explainer: God hears Manoah, and the angel comes again to the woman while she is in the field. Manoah is not with her. The angel appears to the woman twice, giving her the primary role.

verses.text: The woman hurried and ran, and told her husband, and said to him, “Behold, the man who came to me that day has appeared to me,”
verses.explainer: The woman runs to get Manoah: 'The man who appeared to me the other day is here!' She brings Manoah to the angel.

verses.text:
Manoah arose, and followed his wife, and came to the man, and said to him, “Are you the man who spoke to my wife?”
He said, “I am.”
verses.explainer: Manoah asks: 'Are you the man who talked to my wife?' 'I am,' the angel says. Manoah's questions will reveal his limited understanding compared to his wife's intuition.

verses.text: Manoah said, “Now let your words happen. What shall the child’s way of life and mission be?”
verses.explainer: Manoah asks: 'When your words are fulfilled, what is to be the rule that governs the boy's life and work?' He wants practical parenting instructions.

verses.text: Yahweh’s angel said to Manoah, “Of all that I said to the woman let her beware.
verses.explainer: The angel repeats to Manoah the same instructions already given to the woman. No new information is added -- the angel's original message to the woman was complete.

verses.text: She may not eat of anything that comes of the vine, neither let her drink wine or strong drink, nor eat any unclean thing. Let her observe all that I commanded her.”
verses.explainer: She must not eat anything from the vine, drink wine or fermented drink, or eat anything unclean. She must do everything I have commanded her. The instructions focus on the mother's behavior.

verses.text: Manoah said to Yahweh’s angel, “Please stay with us, that we may make a young goat ready for you.”
verses.explainer: Manoah offers to prepare a young goat for the angel. He still thinks his visitor is an ordinary prophet or man of God.

verses.text: Yahweh’s angel said to Manoah, “Though you detain me, I won’t eat your bread. If you will prepare a burnt offering, you must offer it to Yahweh.” For Manoah didn’t know that he was Yahweh’s angel.
verses.explainer: The angel replies: 'Even though you detain me, I will not eat your food. But if you prepare a burnt offering, offer it to the LORD.' The angel redirects the meal toward worship.

verses.text: Manoah said to Yahweh’s angel, “What is your name, that when your words happen, we may honor you?”
verses.explainer: Manoah asks: 'What is your name, so that we may honor you when your word comes true?' He wants to know the messenger's identity.

verses.text: Yahweh’s angel said to him, “Why do you ask about my name, since it is incomprehensible?”
verses.explainer: The angel responds: 'Why do you ask my name? It is beyond understanding.' The Hebrew word peli ('beyond understanding' or 'wonderful') echoes the divine name in Isaiah 9:6.

crossReferences.text: Jacob also asked for the divine visitor's name and was refused: 'Why do you ask my name?' The same pattern of withheld divine identity.

verses.text: So Manoah took the young goat with the meal offering, and offered it on the rock to Yahweh. Then the angel did an amazing thing as Manoah and his wife watched.
verses.explainer: Manoah takes the goat and grain offering and sacrifices them on a rock to the LORD. As the flame blazes up, the angel ascends in the flame -- a theophany.

verses.text: For when the flame went up toward the sky from off the altar, Yahweh’s angel ascended in the flame of the altar. Manoah and his wife watched; and they fell on their faces to the ground.
verses.explainer: As the flame blazes up from the altar toward heaven, the angel of the LORD ascends in the flame. Manoah and his wife fall with their faces to the ground.

verses.text: But Yahweh’s angel didn’t appear to Manoah or to his wife any more. Then Manoah knew that he was Yahweh’s angel.
verses.explainer: The angel does not appear again. Manoah then realizes that it was the angel of the LORD. His recognition comes after the theophany, not before.

verses.text: Manoah said to his wife, “We shall surely die, because we have seen God.”
verses.explainer: Manoah says to his wife: 'We are doomed to die! We have seen God!' His terror is based on the tradition that no one can see God and live.

crossReferences.text: God told Moses: 'No one may see me and live.' Manoah's fear is based on this tradition.

verses.text: But his wife said to him, “If Yahweh were pleased to kill us, he wouldn’t have received a burnt offering and a meal offering at our hand, and he wouldn’t have shown us all these things, nor would he have told us such things as these at this time.”
verses.explainer: His wife responds with remarkable theological clarity: 'If the LORD had meant to kill us, he would not have accepted our offering and shown us all these things.' Her logic is flawless.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Manoah's wife shows consistently better theological judgment than her husband throughout this chapter. She grasps the situation's meaning while he remains confused and afraid. The unnamed woman is the chapter's true protagonist.

thenVsNow.then: Manoah's wife demonstrates that theological insight is not limited by social status or gender. Her logical argument -- 'God would not show us these things just to kill us' -- is superior theology, calmly offered to a panicking husband.
thenVsNow.now: Manoah's wife demonstrates that theological insight is not limited by social status or gender. Her logical argument -- 'God would not show us these things just to kill us' -- is superior theology, calmly offered to a panicking husband.

verses.text: The woman bore a son, and named him Samson. The child grew, and Yahweh blessed him.
verses.explainer: The woman gives birth to a son and names him Samson. He grows up and the LORD blesses him. The promised child arrives.

verses.text: Yahweh’s Spirit began to move him in Mahaneh Dan, between Zorah and Eshtaol.
verses.explainer: The Spirit of the LORD begins to stir Samson while he is in Mahaneh Dan, between Zorah and Eshtaol. The Spirit's activity is the prelude to Samson's career as judge.

explainer.summary: Chapter 14 begins Samson's adult career with his desire for a Philistine woman, a lion-killing incident, a riddle contest, and a marriage that collapses in betrayal. The chapter establishes Samson's character: supernaturally strong but personally weak, Spirit-empowered but desire-driven, and unwilling to observe the Nazirite vow that defines his calling.
themes: Desire overruling divine calling
God working through flawed human choices
The vulnerability of the strong
Nazirite vow violations as progressive spiritual decline
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 14 introduces the tragic pattern of Samson's life: Spirit-empowered strength combined with personal weakness, especially regarding women. Each chapter will show Samson violating another aspect of his Nazirite vow until nothing remains.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Samson 'sees' a woman and wants her
wordStudies.meaning: Samson 'sees' a woman and wants her. The verb connects to the book's refrain: 'everyone did what was right in their own eyes.' Samson literally lives by what he sees, following his eyes rather than his calling.
wordStudies.significance: Samson 'sees' a woman and wants her. The verb connects to the book's refrain: 'everyone did what was

wordStudies.translatedAs: The same Spirit that empowered Othniel and Gideon
wordStudies.meaning: The same Spirit that empowered Othniel and Gideon now comes upon Samson. But Samson uses Spirit-given strength for personal vengeance rather than national deliverance, showing that divine empowerment does not guarantee moral behavior.
wordStudies.significance: The same Spirit that empowered Othniel and Gideon now comes upon Samson. But Samson uses Spirit-give

verses.text: Samson went down to Timnah, and saw a woman in Timnah of the daughters of the Philistines.
verses.explainer: Samson goes down to Timnah and sees a young Philistine woman. The verb 'sees' introduces a pattern: Samson is ruled by what his eyes desire rather than by God's calling.

verses.text: He came up, and told his father and his mother, and said, “I have seen a woman in Timnah of the daughters of the Philistines. Now therefore get her for me as my wife.”
verses.explainer: Samson tells his parents: 'I have seen a Philistine woman in Timnah. Now get her for me as my wife.' He demands rather than requests. His parents' role is reduced to executing his desires.

verses.text:
Then his father and his mother said to him, “Isn’t there a woman among your brothers’ daughters, or among all my people, that you go to take a wife of the uncircumcised Philistines?”
Samson said to his father, “Get her for me, for she pleases me well.”
verses.explainer: His parents object: 'Isn't there an acceptable woman among your relatives or among all our people? Must you go to the uncircumcised Philistines?' They correctly identify the problem.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Marrying outside the covenant community was prohibited (Deuteronomy 7:3), but Samson treats the prohibition as irrelevant. His parents' objection is theologically correct, but they ultimately comply with his demands.

verses.text: But his father and his mother didn’t know that it was of Yahweh; for he sought an occasion against the Philistines. Now at that time the Philistines ruled over Israel.
verses.explainer: But his parents do not know that this is from the LORD, who is seeking an occasion to confront the Philistines. God works through Samson's flawed desires to accomplish divine purposes.

thenVsNow.then: God working through Samson's sinful desire does not endorse that desire. Divine sovereignty encompasses human freedom without approving human sin. God can use broken instruments without sanctifying the brokenness.
thenVsNow.now: God working through Samson's sinful desire does not endorse that desire. Divine sovereignty encompasses human freedom without approving human sin. God can use broken instruments without sanctifying the brokenness.

verses.text: Then went Samson down with his father and his mother to Timnah, and came to the vineyards of Timnah; and behold, a young lion roared against him.
verses.explainer: Samson goes down to Timnah with his parents. Near the vineyards, a young lion comes roaring toward him. A Nazirite near vineyards already hints at boundary violations.

verses.text: Yahweh’s Spirit came mightily on him, and he tore him as he would have torn a young goat; and he had nothing in his hand, but he didn’t tell his father or his mother what he had done.
verses.explainer: The Spirit of the LORD comes powerfully upon him and he tears the lion apart with his bare hands, as one might tear a young goat. He does not tell his parents.

verses.text: He went down, and talked with the woman, and she pleased Samson well.
verses.explainer: Samson talks with the woman, and she pleases him. His attraction is confirmed; the marriage arrangement proceeds. 'She pleases him' -- again, his eyes and desires drive his choices.

verses.text: After a while he returned to take her; and he went over to see the carcass of the lion; and behold, there was a swarm of bees in the body of the lion, and honey.
verses.explainer: Sometime later, returning to marry her, Samson turns aside to look at the lion's carcass. In it is a swarm of bees and honey. A dead animal -- contact with which violates his Nazirite vow.

crossReferences.text: The Nazirite must not go near a dead body, not even for father or mother. Samson's contact with the lion carcass directly violates this command.

verses.text: He took it into his hands, and went on, eating as he went. He came to his father and mother, and gave to them, and they ate, but he didn’t tell them that he had taken the honey out of the lion’s body.
verses.explainer: Samson scoops out the honey with his hands, eating it as he walks. He gives some to his parents without telling them its source. He violates his Nazirite vow (touching a corpse) and implicates his parents.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Honey from a lion's carcass created a double violation: Nazirites could not touch dead bodies (Numbers 6:6-7), and honey from a corpse was ritually unclean. Samson's indifference to these boundaries reveals his casual attitude toward his consecration.

verses.text: His father went down to the woman; and Samson made a feast there, for the young men used to do so.
verses.explainer: Samson's father goes to the woman, and Samson holds a feast there, as was customary for young men. The Hebrew word for 'feast' (mishteh) implies a drinking party -- another Nazirite violation.

verses.text: When they saw him, they brought thirty companions to be with him.
verses.explainer: When the Philistines see him, they provide thirty companions to be with him. These 'companions' may be more like guards than friends.

verses.text: Samson said to them, “Let me tell you a riddle now. If you can declare it to me within the seven days of the feast, and find it out, then I will give you thirty linen garments and thirty changes of clothing;
verses.explainer: Samson proposes a riddle: 'If you can give me the answer within seven days of the feast, I will give you thirty linen garments and thirty sets of clothes.' A wager to make the feast interesting.

verses.text:
but if you can’t declare it to me, then you shall give me thirty linen garments and thirty changes of clothing.”
They said to him, “Tell us your riddle, that we may hear it.”
verses.explainer: But if they cannot, they must give him thirty linen garments and thirty sets of clothes. The stakes are even on both sides.

verses.text:
He said to them,
“Out of the eater came out food.
Out of the strong came out sweetness.”
They couldn’t in three days declare the riddle.
verses.explainer: Samson's riddle: 'Out of the eater, something to eat; out of the strong, something sweet.' The riddle is based on his private experience with the lion -- it's unsolvable without insider knowledge.

verses.text: On the seventh day, they said to Samson’s wife, “Entice your husband, that he may declare to us the riddle, lest we burn you and your father’s house with fire. Have you called us to impoverish us? Isn’t that so?”
verses.explainer: On the fourth day, the Philistines threaten Samson's wife: 'Coax your husband into explaining the riddle, or we will burn you and your father's household to death. Did you invite us here to steal our property?'

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The threat to burn Samson's wife and her family was not empty; it is carried out in Judges 15:6. The Philistines used terror to maintain social control.

verses.text:
Samson’s wife wept before him, and said, “You just hate me, and don’t love me. You’ve told a riddle to the children of my people, and haven’t told it to me.”
He said to her, “Behold, I haven’t told my father or my mother, so why should I tell you?”
verses.explainer: Samson's wife weeps before him: 'You hate me! You don't really love me. You've given my people a riddle but haven't told me the answer.' Her tears are a weapon -- and a pattern Delilah will repeat.

verses.text: She wept before him the seven days, while their feast lasted; and on the seventh day, he told her, because she pressed him severely; and she told the riddle to the children of her people.
verses.explainer: She cries throughout the remaining days of the feast. On the seventh day he tells her because she keeps pressing him. She then tells the answer to her people. Samson cannot resist weeping women.

crossReferences.text: Delilah will use the same tactic -- persistent nagging -- to extract the secret of Samson's strength. Samson never learns from this earlier pattern.

thenVsNow.then: Samson's inability to keep secrets from women who weep reveals a profound irony: the strongest man in Israel is powerless against emotional manipulation. Physical strength does not compensate for emotional vulnerability.
thenVsNow.now: Samson's inability to keep secrets from women who weep reveals a profound irony: the strongest man in Israel is powerless against emotional manipulation. Physical strength does not compensate for emotional vulnerability.

verses.text:
The men of the city said to him on the seventh day before the sun went down, “What is sweeter than honey? What is stronger than a lion?”
He said to them,
“If you hadn’t plowed with my heifer,
you wouldn’t have found out my riddle.”
verses.explainer: Before sunset on the seventh day, the men give the answer: 'What is sweeter than honey? What is stronger than a lion?' Samson knows immediately that they got the answer from his wife.

verses.text: Yahweh’s Spirit came mightily on him, and he went down to Ashkelon, and struck thirty men of them, and took their plunder, then gave the changes of clothing to those who declared the riddle. His anger burned, and he went up to his father’s house.
verses.explainer: The Spirit of the LORD comes powerfully upon Samson. He goes down to Ashkelon, strikes down thirty men, strips their belongings, and gives their clothes to those who solved the riddle. He is furious.

verses.text: But Samson’s wife was given to his companion who had been his friend.
verses.explainer: Samson's wife is given to one of his companions. The marriage is dissolved before it is consummated. This betrayal will fuel Samson's next campaign of vengeance in chapter 15.

explainer.summary: Chapter 15 escalates the cycle of revenge between Samson and the Philistines. After his wife is given away, Samson destroys the grain harvest with burning foxes, triggering the Philistines to burn his wife and father-in-law. Samson retaliates with more violence. His own people try to hand him to the Philistines, but the Spirit empowers him to kill a thousand men with a donkey's jawbone.
themes: The endless cycle of revenge
Personal grievance driving national conflict
God's power working through deeply flawed agents
Israel's acceptance of oppression
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 15 shows Samson at his most powerful physically and his most compromised morally. The contrast between divine empowerment and personal motivation could not be sharper. His own people's attempt to hand him over foreshadows Israel's capitulation.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To avenge or take vengeance
wordStudies.meaning: To avenge or take vengeance. Samson's career is driven by personal revenge rather than national liberation. God channels this revenge toward His purposes, but Samson's motive remains self-centered.
wordStudies.significance: To avenge or take vengeance. Samson's career is driven by personal revenge rather than national libe

wordStudies.translatedAs: The divine empowerment that comes upon Samson for
wordStudies.meaning: The divine empowerment that comes upon Samson for extraordinary feats. The Spirit breaks the ropes and enables the jawbone victory, but Samson's use of this power is consistently for personal vengeance rather than communal deliverance.
wordStudies.significance: The divine empowerment that comes upon Samson for extraordinary feats. The Spirit breaks the ropes a

verses.text:
But after a while, in the time of wheat harvest, Samson visited his wife with a young goat. He said, “I will go in to my wife’s room.”
But her father wouldn’t allow him to go in.
verses.explainer: Later, during the wheat harvest, Samson takes a young goat and goes to visit his wife. He says, 'I'm going to my wife's room.' But her father refuses to let him in.

verses.text: Her father said, “I most certainly thought that you utterly hated her; therefore I gave her to your companion. Isn’t her younger sister more beautiful than she? Please take her, instead.”
verses.explainer: The father explains: 'I was so sure you hated her that I gave her to your companion. Isn't her younger sister more attractive? Take her instead.' The father treats daughters as interchangeable commodities.

verses.text: Samson said to them, “This time I will be blameless in the case of the Philistines, when I harm them.”
