verses.explainer: Samson declares: 'This time I have a right to get even with the Philistines; I will really harm them.' Personal grievance becomes his justification for violence.

thenVsNow.then: Samson's claim to 'a right to get even' reveals the endless nature of revenge cycles. Each act of retaliation seems justified to the one who commits it, but each also demands a counter-response.
thenVsNow.now: Samson's claim to 'a right to get even' reveals the endless nature of revenge cycles. Each act of retaliation seems justified to the one who commits it, but each also demands a counter-response.

verses.text: Samson went and caught three hundred foxes, and took torches, and turned tail to tail, and put a torch in the middle between every two tails.
verses.explainer: Samson catches three hundred foxes, ties them tail to tail in pairs, fastens a torch to every pair of tails, and releases them into the Philistine grain fields. An act of agricultural terrorism.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Agricultural sabotage was devastating in an agrarian economy. Destroying the grain harvest meant starvation for the entire population. Samson's act was not a military operation but an economic attack on civilians.

verses.text: When he had set the torches on fire, he let them go into the standing grain of the Philistines, and burned up both the shocks and the standing grain, and also the olive groves.
verses.explainer: The foxes with burning tails set fire to the standing grain, the shocked grain, the vineyards, and the olive groves. The Philistine harvest is destroyed -- an economic catastrophe.

verses.text:
Then the Philistines said, “Who has done this?”
They said, “Samson, the son-in-law of the Timnite, because he has taken his wife, and given her to his companion.” The Philistines came up, and burned her and her father with fire.
verses.explainer: When the Philistines ask who did this, they learn it was Samson, son-in-law of the Timnite, because his wife was given to his companion. They burn her and her father to death -- the very fate she was threatened with.

verses.text: Samson said to them, “If you behave like this, surely I will take revenge on you, and after that I will cease.”
verses.explainer: Samson responds: 'Since you've acted like this, I swear I won't stop until I get my revenge.' The cycle of violence escalates beyond any possibility of resolution.

verses.text: He struck them hip and thigh with a great slaughter; and he went down and lived in the cave in Etam’s rock.
verses.explainer: Samson attacks the Philistines viciously, slaughtering many. Then he goes down to live in a cave in the rock of Etam. He becomes a fugitive guerrilla fighter.

verses.text: Then the Philistines went up, encamped in Judah, and spread themselves in Lehi.
verses.explainer: The Philistines go up and camp in Judah, spreading out near Lehi. They bring their army into Israelite territory to find one man.

verses.text:
The men of Judah said, “Why have you come up against us?”
They said, “We have come up to bind Samson, to do to him as he has done to us.”
verses.explainer: The men of Judah ask the Philistines: 'Why have you come to fight us?' They answer: 'We have come to take Samson prisoner and do to him as he did to us.' Israel's own tribe must deal with Samson.

verses.text:
Then three thousand men of Judah went down to the cave in Etam’s rock, and said to Samson, “Don’t you know that the Philistines are rulers over us? What then is this that you have done to us?”
He said to them, “As they did to me, so I have done to them.”
verses.explainer: Three thousand men of Judah go to Samson's cave. They confront him: 'Don't you realize the Philistines rule over us? What have you done to us?' His own people see him as the problem, not the solution.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Judah's willingness to hand Samson over to the Philistines shows how thoroughly the southern tribes had accepted Philistine domination. They preferred accommodation to resistance.

crossReferences.text: God was 'seeking an occasion' against the Philistines through Samson. Judah's submission shows how desperately Israel needed deliverance.

thenVsNow.then: Judah's people would rather hand over their deliverer than fight their oppressor. When people accept oppression as normal, anyone who challenges the status quo becomes a greater threat than the oppressor.
thenVsNow.now: Judah's people would rather hand over their deliverer than fight their oppressor. When people accept oppression as normal, anyone who challenges the status quo becomes a greater threat than the oppressor.

verses.text:
They said to him, “We have come down to bind you, that we may deliver you into the hand of the Philistines.”
Samson said to them, “Swear to me that you will not attack me yourselves.”
verses.explainer: They say they've come to tie him up and hand him over to the Philistines. Samson makes them promise not to kill him themselves. He allows his own people to bind him.

verses.text: They spoke to him, saying, “No; but we will bind you securely, and deliver you into their hands; but surely we will not kill you.” They bound him with two new ropes, and brought him up from the rock.
verses.explainer: They bind him with two new ropes and lead him up from the rock. Samson submits to his own people while preparing to fight the real enemy.

verses.text: When he came to Lehi, the Philistines shouted as they met him. Then Yahweh’s Spirit came mightily on him, and the ropes that were on his arms became as flax that was burned with fire, and his bands dropped from off his hands.
verses.explainer: As Samson approaches the Philistines at Lehi, they come toward him shouting. The Spirit of the LORD comes powerfully upon him. The ropes on his arms become like charred flax, and the bindings drop from his hands.

crossReferences.text: The Spirit empowers Samson for extraordinary physical feats, as when he tore the lion apart.

verses.text: He found a fresh jawbone of a donkey, put out his hand, took it, and struck a thousand men with it.
verses.explainer: Finding a fresh jawbone of a donkey, Samson picks it up and strikes down a thousand men. A fresh jawbone -- still moist and strong, unlike a dry brittle one -- becomes a devastating weapon.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Touching a donkey's jawbone -- a dead animal's bone -- violates the Nazirite prohibition against contact with dead bodies. Samson's greatest military victory involves another violation of his consecration.

verses.text: Samson said, “With the jawbone of a donkey, heaps on heaps; with the jawbone of a donkey I have struck a thousand men.”
verses.explainer: Samson declares: 'With a donkey's jawbone I have made donkeys of them. With a donkey's jawbone I have killed a thousand men.' His victory song is a pun-filled boast.

verses.text: When he had finished speaking, he threw the jawbone out of his hand; and that place was called Ramath Lehi.
verses.explainer: He throws away the jawbone and names the place Ramath Lehi ('jawbone hill'). The battlefield receives a memorial name.

verses.text: He was very thirsty, and called on Yahweh, and said, “You have given this great deliverance by the hand of your servant; and now shall I die of thirst, and fall into the hands of the uncircumcised?”
verses.explainer: Samson is desperately thirsty and cries out to the LORD: 'You have given your servant this great victory. Must I now die of thirst and fall into the hands of the uncircumcised?' His first prayer -- and it's for water, not wisdom.

crossReferences.text: The psalmist also cries out in thirst: 'My strength is dried up like a potsherd, my tongue sticks to the roof of my mouth.'

thenVsNow.then: Samson's prayer for water after battle is his most genuine moment of dependence on God. Even the most self-reliant eventually encounter needs they cannot meet alone.
thenVsNow.now: Samson's prayer for water after battle is his most genuine moment of dependence on God. Even the most self-reliant eventually encounter needs they cannot meet alone.

verses.text: But God split the hollow place that is in Lehi, and water came out of it. When he had drunk, his spirit came again, and he revived. Therefore its name was called En Hakkore, which is in Lehi, to this day.
verses.explainer: God opens up a hollow place in Lehi, and water comes out. Samson drinks, his strength returns, and he revives. The spring is named En Hakkore ('spring of the caller').

verses.text: He judged Israel twenty years in the days of the Philistines.
verses.explainer: Samson leads Israel for twenty years in the days of the Philistines. Unlike other judges, his tenure does not end with peace -- the Philistine oppression continues throughout.

explainer.summary: Chapter 16 tells Samson's final story: his visit to a Gaza prostitute, his doomed relationship with Delilah, the loss of his strength when his hair is shaved, his humiliation as a blind prisoner, and his final act of self-sacrifice that kills more Philistines in his death than in his life. It is simultaneously the most tragic and the most redemptive chapter in Samson's saga.
themes: The deadly consequences of moral compromise
Strength and weakness coexisting in the same person
God's grace to the undeserving
Redemption through self-sacrifice
explainer.contextInBook: Samson's story ends the main judge narratives. His death -- killing more in death than in life -- tragically summarizes his wasted potential. He 'began' the deliverance from the Philistines (13:5) but never completed it. That task would fall to Samuel and David.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The name may derive from the Hebrew dalal, meaning
wordStudies.meaning: The name may derive from the Hebrew dalal, meaning 'to weaken' or 'to bring low.' If so, her name foreshadows her role. She is the instrument of Samson's downfall, though she is also a tool of the Philistine rulers.
