wordStudies.translatedAs: The departure of the LORD from Samson is the theol
wordStudies.meaning: The departure of the LORD from Samson is the theological climax of the story. Samson does not know God has left -- suggesting a gradual departure that Samson never noticed because he relied on his own strength rather than on God's presence.
wordStudies.significance: The departure of the LORD from Samson is the theological climax of the story. Samson does not know G

verses.text: Samson went to Gaza, and saw there a prostitute, and went in to her.
verses.explainer: Samson goes to Gaza and sees a prostitute. He goes in to spend the night with her. The Nazirite consecrated to God from the womb visits a prostitute in the heart of Philistine territory.

verses.text: The Gazites were told, “Samson is here!” They surrounded him, and laid wait for him all night in the gate of the city, and were quiet all the night, saying, “Wait until morning light, then we will kill him.”
verses.explainer: The people of Gaza are told: 'Samson is here!' They surround the place and lie in wait for him all night at the city gate, planning to kill him at dawn.

verses.text: Samson lay until midnight, and arose at midnight, and laid hold of the doors of the gate of the city, and the two posts, and plucked them up, bar and all, and put them on his shoulders, and carried them up to the top of the mountain that is before Hebron.
verses.explainer: Samson lies there until midnight, then gets up and tears out the city gate -- posts, bar, and all -- puts them on his shoulders, and carries them to a hilltop facing Hebron. An impossible feat of strength.

verses.text: It came to pass afterward, that he loved a woman in the valley of Sorek, whose name was Delilah.
verses.explainer: Later, Samson falls in love with a woman in the Valley of Sorek. Her name is Delilah. The Valley of Sorek was on the border between Israelite and Philistine territory.

verses.text: The lords of the Philistines came up to her, and said to her, “Entice him, and see in which his great strength lies, and by what means we may prevail against him, that we may bind him to afflict him; and we will each give you eleven hundred pieces of silver.”
verses.explainer: The rulers of the Philistines approach Delilah: 'See if you can lure him into showing you the secret of his great strength.' Each ruler offers eleven hundred shekels of silver -- a fortune.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Eleven hundred shekels from each ruler (five rulers total) meant 5,500 shekels of silver -- an enormous sum. Delilah's betrayal was handsomely financed. The Philistines considered Samson worth any price.

verses.text: Delilah said to Samson, “Please tell me where your great strength lies, and what you might be bound to afflict you.”
verses.explainer: Delilah asks Samson: 'Tell me the secret of your great strength and how you can be tied up and subdued.' Her question is so transparent that Samson's willingness to play along is astonishing.

verses.text: Samson said to her, “If they bind me with seven green cords that were never dried, then shall I become weak, and be as another man.”
verses.explainer: Samson lies: 'If anyone ties me with seven fresh bowstrings that have not been dried, I'll become as weak as any other man.' His first deception -- he treats the interrogation as a game.

verses.text: Then the lords of the Philistines brought up to her seven green cords which had not been dried, and she bound him with them.
verses.explainer: The Philistine rulers bring her seven fresh bowstrings, and she ties him up while men hide in the room. The trap is set.

verses.text: Now she had an ambush waiting in the inner room. She said to him, “The Philistines are on you, Samson!” He broke the cords, as a string of tow is broken when it touches the fire. So his strength was not known.
verses.explainer: She calls: 'Samson, the Philistines are upon you!' He snaps the bowstrings as easily as string touched by fire. The secret of his strength is not revealed.

verses.text: Delilah said to Samson, “Behold, you have mocked me, and told me lies: now please tell me with which you might be bound.”
verses.explainer: Delilah confronts him: 'You have made a fool of me; you lied to me. Now tell me how you can be tied.' She drops all pretense of affection and demands information.

verses.text: He said to her, “If they only bind me with new ropes with which no work has been done, then shall I become weak, and be as another man.”
verses.explainer: Samson lies again: 'If anyone ties me securely with new ropes that have never been used, I'll become as weak as any other man.' His second false answer escalates the game.

verses.text: So Delilah took new ropes, and bound him therewith, and said to him, “The Philistines are on you, Samson!” The ambush was waiting in the inner room. He broke them off his arms like a thread.
verses.explainer: Delilah ties him with new ropes and again shouts: 'Samson, the Philistines are upon you!' Again he snaps the ropes off his arms like thread.

verses.text:
Delilah said to Samson, “Until now, you have mocked me and told me lies. Tell me with what you might be bound.”
He said to her, “If you weave the seven locks of my head with the web.”
verses.explainer: Delilah protests: 'All this time you have been making a fool of me and lying. Tell me how you can be tied.' Her persistence should alarm him, but it doesn't.

verses.text: She fastened it with the pin, and said to him, “The Philistines are on you, Samson!” He awakened out of his sleep, and plucked away the pin of the beam, and the web.
verses.explainer: Samson tells her: 'If you weave the seven braids of my hair into the fabric on the loom and tighten it with the pin, I'll become as weak as any other man.' He's getting dangerously close to the truth -- his hair is now involved.

verses.text: She said to him, “How can you say, ‘I love you,’ when your heart is not with me? You have mocked me these three times, and have not told me where your great strength lies.”
verses.explainer: While he sleeps, Delilah weaves his braids into the fabric. When she calls out, he pulls up the loom pin and the fabric. But the progression toward truth is clear -- each lie comes closer to the real answer.

verses.text: When she pressed him daily with her words, and urged him, his soul was troubled to death.
verses.explainer: Delilah says: 'How can you say you love me when you won't confide in me? This is the third time you have made a fool of me.' She uses the same emotional manipulation that worked on his first wife.

verses.text: He told her all his heart, and said to her, “No razor has ever come on my head; for I have been a Nazirite to God from my mother’s womb. If I am shaved, then my strength will go from me, and I will become weak, and be like any other man.”
verses.explainer: With such nagging she prods him day after day until he is sick to death. So he tells her everything: 'No razor has ever been used on my head because I have been a Nazirite dedicated to God from birth. If my head were shaved, my strength would leave me.'

verses.text: When Delilah saw that he had told her all his heart, she sent and called for the lords of the Philistines, saying, “Come up this once, for he has told me all his heart.” Then the lords of the Philistines came up to her, and brought the money in their hand.
verses.explainer: When Delilah realizes he has told her everything, she sends word to the Philistine rulers: 'Come back one more time; he has told me everything.' They come with the silver in hand.

verses.text: She made him sleep on her knees; and she called for a man, and shaved off the seven locks of his head; and she began to afflict him, and his strength went from him.
verses.explainer: She lulls Samson to sleep on her lap, calls a man in, and has the seven braids of his head shaved off. His strength begins to leave him. The Nazirite vow is finally broken completely.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Samson's hair was not the source of his strength -- it was the symbol of his Nazirite vow. When the hair was cut, it symbolized the final breaking of his consecration to God. The power came from God's Spirit, not from hair.

verses.text:
She said, “The Philistines are upon you, Samson!”
He awoke out of his sleep, and said, “I will go out as at other times, and shake myself free.” But he didn’t know that Yahweh had departed from him.
verses.explainer: She calls: 'Samson, the Philistines are upon you!' He wakes and thinks: 'I'll go out as before and shake myself free.' But he does not know that the LORD has left him. The most devastating sentence in the chapter.

thenVsNow.then: Samson did not know that the LORD had left him -- the most terrifying verse in his story. Spiritual departure can be so gradual that we don't notice God's absence until we reach for power that is no longer there.
thenVsNow.now: Samson did not know that the LORD had left him -- the most terrifying verse in his story. Spiritual departure can be so gradual that we don't notice God's absence until we reach for power that is no longer there.

verses.text: The Philistines laid hold on him, and put out his eyes; and they brought him down to Gaza, and bound him with fetters of brass; and he ground at the mill in the prison.
verses.explainer: The Philistines seize him, gouge out his eyes, and take him down to Gaza. They bind him with bronze shackles, and he grinds grain in the prison. The strongman is blinded, chained, and set to women's work.

verses.text: However the hair of his head began to grow again after he was shaved.
verses.explainer: But the hair on his head begins to grow again. This detail, almost a throwaway line, plants the seed of Samson's final act.

verses.text: The lords of the Philistines gathered them together to offer a great sacrifice to Dagon their god, and to rejoice; for they said, “Our god has delivered Samson our enemy into our hand.”
verses.explainer: The rulers of the Philistines assemble to offer a great sacrifice to their god Dagon, celebrating: 'Our god has delivered Samson our enemy into our hands.' They credit Dagon for Samson's defeat.

verses.text: When the people saw him, they praised their god; for they said, “Our god has delivered our enemy and the destroyer of our country, who has slain many of us, into our hand.”
verses.explainer: When the people see Samson, they praise their god: 'Our god has delivered our enemy into our hands, the one who laid waste our land and multiplied our slain.' The celebration is a direct challenge to the LORD.

verses.text: When their hearts were merry, they said, “Call for Samson, that he may entertain us.” They called for Samson out of the prison; and he performed before them. They set him between the pillars;
verses.explainer: While in high spirits, they shout: 'Bring out Samson to entertain us.' So they call Samson out of prison and he performs for them. The champion of Israel is now a Philistine court jester.

verses.text: and Samson said to the boy who held him by the hand, “Allow me to feel the pillars whereupon the house rests, that I may lean on them.”
verses.explainer: Samson says to the servant who leads him by the hand: 'Put me where I can feel the pillars that support the temple, so I can lean against them.' He positions himself for his final act.

verses.text: Now the house was full of men and women; and all the lords of the Philistines were there; and there were on the roof about three thousand men and women, who saw while Samson performed.
verses.explainer: The temple is packed with men and women, and about three thousand people are on the roof watching Samson perform. The building is at maximum capacity.

verses.text: Samson called to Yahweh, and said, “Lord Yahweh, remember me, please, and strengthen me, please, only this once, God, that I may be at once avenged of the Philistines for my two eyes.”
verses.explainer: Samson prays to the LORD: 'Sovereign LORD, remember me. Please, God, strengthen me just once more, and let me with one blow get revenge on the Philistines for my two eyes.' His final prayer is for revenge -- but God answers it.

crossReferences.text: Despite his failures, Samson is listed among the heroes of faith in Hebrews 11, suggesting God honored his final act of faith.

thenVsNow.then: Samson's final prayer -- for revenge, not repentance -- is nonetheless answered by God. Even flawed prayers, offered in genuine dependence, receive divine response. God's grace exceeds our theology.
thenVsNow.now: Samson's final prayer -- for revenge, not repentance -- is nonetheless answered by God. Even flawed prayers, offered in genuine dependence, receive divine response. God's grace exceeds our theology.

verses.text: Samson took hold of the two middle pillars on which the house rested, and leaned on them, the one with his right hand, and the other with his left.
verses.explainer: Samson reaches toward the two central pillars on which the temple stands, bracing himself against them with his right hand on one and his left hand on the other.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Ancient Philistine temples were supported by two central wooden pillars on stone bases. Archaeological excavations at Tell Qasile have confirmed this architecture. A person positioned between the pillars could, with extraordinary strength, displace them and collapse the roof.

verses.text: Samson said, “Let me die with the Philistines!” He bowed himself with all his might; and the house fell on the lords, and on all the people who were therein. So the dead that he killed at his death were more than those who he killed in his life.
verses.explainer: Samson declares: 'Let me die with the Philistines!' He pushes with all his might, and the temple collapses on the rulers and all the people in it. He kills more Philistines in his death than in his life.

thenVsNow.then: Samson kills more in death than in life, making his death his greatest victory. This paradox -- strength in weakness, triumph through sacrifice -- anticipates the cross, where the greatest victory came through apparent defeat.
thenVsNow.now: Samson kills more in death than in life, making his death his greatest victory. This paradox -- strength in weakness, triumph through sacrifice -- anticipates the cross, where the greatest victory came through apparent defeat.

verses.text: Then his brothers and all the house of his father came down, and took him, and brought him up, and buried him between Zorah and Eshtaol in the burial site of Manoah his father. He judged Israel twenty years.
verses.explainer: Samson's brothers and his father's whole family come to get his body. They bury him between Zorah and Eshtaol, in the tomb of his father Manoah. He had led Israel twenty years.

crossReferences.text: The angel said Samson would 'begin' to deliver Israel from the Philistines. He began but never completed the task.

explainer.summary: Chapter 17 introduces the book's epilogue by telling how Micah, an Ephraimite, creates a private shrine with stolen-then-returned money, installs his son as priest, and then hires a wandering Levite as his personal chaplain. Every element violates Israelite law while maintaining a veneer of religious propriety.
themes: Religious syncretism -- mixing true worship with idolatry
The commodification of religion
Everyone doing what is right in their own eyes
The illusion that correct religious forms validate corrupt content
explainer.contextInBook: Chapters 17-18 form the first half of the book's epilogue, illustrating the religious chaos that characterizes a society without shared moral authority. The story shows how thoroughly Israelite worship has been corrupted.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Small idol figurines kept in homes for divination
wordStudies.meaning: Small idol figurines kept in homes for divination and protection. Rachel stole her father's teraphim (Genesis 31:19), and their presence in Micah's shrine shows the persistence of household idolatry in Israel.
wordStudies.significance: Small idol figurines kept in homes for divination and protection. Rachel stole her father's teraphim

wordStudies.greek: Everyone did as they saw fit
wordStudies.transliteration: Everyone did as they saw fit
wordStudies.translatedAs: The Hebrew literally reads 'each man did what was
wordStudies.meaning: The Hebrew literally reads 'each man did what was right in his own eyes.' This phrase is Judges' thesis statement: without shared moral authority, individual moral autonomy leads to chaos, not freedom.
wordStudies.significance: The Hebrew literally reads 'each man did what was right in his own eyes.' This phrase is Judges' the

verses.text: There was a man of the hill country of Ephraim, whose name was Micah.
verses.explainer: A man named Micah from the hill country of Ephraim confesses to his mother that he stole eleven hundred shekels of silver from her. The same amount each Philistine ruler offered Delilah -- perhaps an intentional echo.

verses.text:
He said to his mother, “The eleven hundred pieces of silver that were taken from you, about which you uttered a curse, and also spoke it in my ears, behold, the silver is with me. I took it.”
His mother said, “May Yahweh bless my son!”
verses.explainer: His mother blesses him for confessing and dedicates the silver to the LORD for making a carved image and a cast idol. She combines genuine devotion to the LORD with blatant violation of the second commandment.

crossReferences.text: The second commandment prohibits making any carved image or likeness for worship -- exactly what Micah's mother commissions.

thenVsNow.then: The mixture of genuine devotion and flagrant idolatry in Micah's household reflects how easily people create 'designer religions' that combine comfortable elements from incompatible belief systems.
thenVsNow.now: The mixture of genuine devotion and flagrant idolatry in Micah's household reflects how easily people create 'designer religions' that combine comfortable elements from incompatible belief systems.

verses.text: He restored the eleven hundred pieces of silver to his mother, then his mother said, “I most certainly dedicate the silver to Yahweh from my hand for my son, to make a carved image and a molten image. Now therefore I will restore it to you.”
verses.explainer: When Micah returns the silver, his mother takes two hundred shekels and gives them to a silversmith to make the idol. She keeps most of the dedicated money -- her devotion is both idolatrous and stingy.

verses.text: When he restored the money to his mother, his mother took two hundred pieces of silver, and gave them to a silversmith, who made a carved image and a molten image out of it. It was in the house of Micah.
verses.explainer: The idol is placed in Micah's house. Micah's household becomes a private shrine, complete with an idol made from stolen money that was supposedly dedicated to God.

verses.text: The man Micah had a house of gods, and he made an ephod, and teraphim, and consecrated one of his sons, who became his priest.
verses.explainer: Micah makes a shrine, an ephod, and some household gods, and installs one of his sons as priest. He creates a complete unauthorized religious system -- shrine, vestments, idols, and clergy.

crossReferences.text: God commanded Israel to worship at the place He would choose (eventually Jerusalem), not in private household shrines.

verses.text: In those days there was no king in Israel. Everyone did that which was right in his own eyes.
verses.explainer: In those days Israel had no king; everyone did as they saw fit. This is the book's refrain and its diagnosis: without authority, moral chaos follows.

verses.text: There was a young man out of Bethlehem Judah, of the family of Judah, who was a Levite; and he lived there.
verses.explainer: A young Levite from Bethlehem in Judah has been living within the clan of Judah. He leaves Bethlehem to find a place to live. A displaced priest seeking employment.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The Levites had no tribal territory; they were supposed to serve at the tabernacle and live in designated Levitical cities. A Levite 'looking for a place to stay' indicates the breakdown of the support system God established for the priestly tribe.

verses.text: The man departed out of the city, out of Bethlehem Judah, to live where he could find a place, and he came to the hill country of Ephraim, to the house of Micah, as he traveled.
verses.explainer: The Levite travels through the hill country of Ephraim and comes to Micah's house. His wandering brings him to the household of an idol-maker.

verses.text:
Micah said to him, “Where did you come from?”
He said to him, “I am a Levite of Bethlehem Judah, and I am looking for a place to live.”
verses.explainer: Micah asks where he's from. The Levite explains he's from Bethlehem and is looking for a place to stay. His priestly credentials make him valuable to Micah.

verses.text: Micah said to him, “Dwell with me, and be to me a father and a priest, and I will give you ten pieces of silver per year, a suit of clothing, and your food.” So the Levite went in.
verses.explainer: Micah offers: 'Live with me and be my father and priest. I'll give you ten shekels of silver a year, plus clothes and food.' He hires a Levite as his personal chaplain.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Hiring a priest with a salary and benefits transforms the priestly office from a divine calling into a commercial transaction. Religion becomes a marketplace, and priests become employees.

verses.text: The Levite was content to dwell with the man; and the young man was to him as one of his sons.
verses.explainer: The Levite agrees to live with Micah and becomes like one of his sons. A Levite -- who should serve at the tabernacle in Shiloh -- becomes the paid priest of a household idol.

verses.text: Micah consecrated the Levite, and the young man became his priest, and was in the house of Micah.
verses.explainer: Micah installs the Levite, who becomes his priest and lives in his house. The private shrine now has a 'legitimate' priest, lending an air of respectability to the idolatry.

verses.text: Then Micah said, “Now know I that Yahweh will do good to me, since I have a Levite as my priest.”
verses.explainer: Micah says: 'Now I know that the LORD will be good to me, since this Levite has become my priest.' He believes that having the right kind of priest validates the wrong kind of worship. Correct form, corrupt content.

thenVsNow.then: Micah's sincere belief that a Levite priest validates his idol worship illustrates a timeless error: confusing religious packaging with genuine faithfulness. Having the right credentials does not sanctify the wrong worship.
thenVsNow.now: Micah's sincere belief that a Levite priest validates his idol worship illustrates a timeless error: confusing religious packaging with genuine faithfulness. Having the right credentials does not sanctify the wrong worship.

explainer.summary: Chapter 18 tells how the tribe of Dan, unable to conquer its assigned territory, migrates north, steals Micah's idol and priest along the way, conquers the peaceful city of Laish, renames it Dan, and establishes an unauthorized worship center. The priest turns out to be a descendant of Moses.
themes: Tribal failure leading to violent displacement
The theft of religion for tribal purposes
Violence against the defenseless
The corruption of Moses' priestly line
explainer.contextInBook: The establishment of Dan's idol worship became a lasting problem in Israel. When Jeroboam later set up golden calves at Dan (1 Kings 12:29), he built on the foundation of this unauthorized worship center.

wordStudies.greek: Jonathan son of Gershom, son of Moses
wordStudies.transliteration: Jonathan son of Gershom, son of Moses
wordStudies.translatedAs: Some manuscripts read 'son of Manasseh' instead of
wordStudies.meaning: Some manuscripts read 'son of Manasseh' instead of 'son of Moses,' with a suspended letter nun inserted to obscure Moses' name. Scribes apparently found it too embarrassing that Moses' own grandson became an idolatrous priest.
wordStudies.significance: Some manuscripts read 'son of Manasseh' instead of 'son of Moses,' with a suspended letter nun inser

wordStudies.translatedAs: A peaceful, prosperous city in the far north, iden
wordStudies.meaning: A peaceful, prosperous city in the far north, identified with modern Tell Dan. Its people lived 'in the manner of the Sidonians' -- prosperously and without military preparedness. Their peaceful lifestyle made them easy prey for the migrating Danites.
wordStudies.significance: A peaceful, prosperous city in the far north, identified with modern Tell Dan. Its people lived 'in

verses.text: In those days there was no king in Israel. In those days the tribe of the Danites sought an inheritance to dwell in; for to that day, their inheritance had not fallen to them among the tribes of Israel.
verses.explainer: In those days Israel has no king. The tribe of Dan is seeking a place of its own to settle because it has not yet received its inheritance among the tribes. Dan's failure to conquer its assigned territory drives the narrative.

verses.text:
The children of Dan sent five men of their family from their whole number, men of valor, from Zorah, and from Eshtaol, to spy out the land, and to search it. They said to them, “Go, explore the land!”
They came to the hill country of Ephraim, to the house of Micah, and lodged there.
verses.explainer: The Danites send five warriors from Zorah and Eshtaol to spy out the land. They come to Micah's house in Ephraim and stay there overnight.

verses.text: When they were by the house of Micah, they knew the voice of the young man the Levite; so they went over there, and said to him, “Who brought you here? What do you do in this place? What do you have here?”
verses.explainer: Near Micah's house, they recognize the young Levite's accent. They ask: 'Who brought you here? What are you doing? Why are you here?' They probe his situation.

verses.text: He said to them, “Thus and thus has Micah dealt with me, and he has hired me, and I am become his priest.”
verses.explainer: The Levite explains his arrangement with Micah: 'He has hired me and I am his priest.' A mercenary priesthood, casually explained.

verses.text: They said to him, “Please ask counsel of God, that we may know whether our way which we go shall be prosperous.”
verses.explainer: The Danites ask the Levite to inquire of God whether their journey will be successful. They use the idolatrous shrine for genuine divine guidance -- the confusion of the era.

verses.text: The priest said to them, “Go in peace. Your way in which you go is before Yahweh.”
verses.explainer: The priest answers: 'Go in peace. Your journey has the LORD's approval.' Whether this oracle is genuine or fabricated, it gives the Danites confidence.

verses.text: Then the five men departed, and came to Laish, and saw the people who were there, how they lived in safety, in the way of the Sidonians, quiet and secure; for there was no one in the land, possessing authority, that might put them to shame in anything, and they were far from the Sidonians, and had no dealings with anyone else.
verses.explainer: The five men travel north to Laish. They find a peaceful, unsuspecting people living securely like the Sidonians -- prosperous and defenseless, far from any ally.

verses.text: They came to their brothers at Zorah and Eshtaol; and their brothers asked them, “What do you say?”
verses.explainer: The spies return and report to their brothers at Zorah and Eshtaol: 'Come, let's attack them! The land is excellent.' They urge immediate action against the defenseless city.

verses.text: They said, “Arise, and let us go up against them; for we have seen the land, and behold, it is very good. Do you stand still? Don’t be slothful to go and to enter in to possess the land.
verses.explainer: They argue: 'Don't hesitate to go and take possession of the land. God has given it into your hands -- a land where nothing is lacking.' They invoke divine sanction for attacking a peaceful city.

verses.text: When you go, you will come to an unsuspecting people, and the land is large; for God has given it into your hand, a place where there is no lack of anything that is in the earth.”
verses.explainer: When you get there, you will find an unsuspecting people and a spacious land. God has given it into your hands. The spies emphasize how easy the conquest will be.

verses.text: The family of the Danites set out from Zorah and Eshtaol, with six hundred men armed with weapons of war.
verses.explainer: Six hundred Danites, armed for battle, set out from Zorah and Eshtaol. An entire tribal military expedition to conquer a single defenseless city.

verses.text: They went up and encamped in Kiriath Jearim, in Judah. Therefore they called that place Mahaneh Dan, to this day. Behold, it is behind Kiriath Jearim.
verses.explainer: They camp at Kiriath-jearim in Judah. The place is still called Mahaneh Dan ('camp of Dan') to this day.

verses.text: They passed from there to the hill country of Ephraim, and came to the house of Micah.
verses.explainer: From there they continue to the hill country of Ephraim and come to Micah's house. The spies remember the shrine they discovered on their earlier visit.

verses.text: Then the five men who went to spy out the country of Laish answered, and said to their brothers, “Do you know that there is in these houses an ephod, and teraphim, and a carved image, and a molten image? Now therefore consider what you have to do.”
verses.explainer: The five spies tell their companions: 'Do you know that in one of these houses there is an ephod, household gods, a carved image, and a cast idol? Now you know what to do.' The theft is premeditated.

verses.text: They went over there, and came to the house of the young Levite man, even to the house of Micah, and asked him how he was doing.
verses.explainer: They turn aside to the Levite's quarters at Micah's house and greet him. The social niceties continue even as they plan to rob him.

verses.text: The six hundred men armed with their weapons of war, who were of the children of Dan, stood by the entrance of the gate.
verses.explainer: The six hundred Danites stand at the entrance to the gate while the five spies enter the house.

verses.text: The five men who went to spy out the land went up, and came in there, and took the engraved image, the ephod, the teraphim, and the molten image; and the priest stood by the entrance of the gate with the six hundred men armed with weapons of war.
verses.explainer: The five men enter and take the carved image, the ephod, the household gods, and the cast idol. The priest stands at the gate with the six hundred armed men.

verses.text: When these went into Micah’s house, and took the engraved image, the ephod, the teraphim, and the molten image, the priest said to them, “What are you doing?”
verses.explainer: When the spies take the sacred objects, the priest asks: 'What are you doing?' The question is plaintive -- he sees his livelihood being carried away.

verses.text: They said to him, “Hold your peace, put your hand on your mouth, and go with us, and be to us a father and a priest. Is it better for you to be priest to the house of one man, or to be priest to a tribe and a family in Israel?”
verses.explainer: They tell him: 'Be quiet! Don't say a word. Come with us, and be our father and priest. Isn't it better to be priest for a whole tribe than for just one household?' The career upgrade silences his objections.

verses.text: The priest’s heart was glad, and he took the ephod, the teraphim, and the engraved image, and went with the people.
verses.explainer: The priest is glad. He takes the ephod, the household gods, and the idol and goes with them. Professional advancement trumps loyalty -- the mercenary priest finds a bigger paycheck.

thenVsNow.then: The Levite's willingness to abandon Micah for a better offer exposes the mercenary nature of his 'ministry.' When religion is a career rather than a calling, loyalty goes to the highest bidder.
thenVsNow.now: The Levite's willingness to abandon Micah for a better offer exposes the mercenary nature of his 'ministry.' When religion is a career rather than a calling, loyalty goes to the highest bidder.

verses.text: So they turned and departed, and put the little ones, the livestock, and the goods before them.
verses.explainer: They put their little children, livestock, and possessions in front, anticipating pursuit from behind. Military precaution shows they know they're stealing.

verses.text: When they were a good way from the house of Micah, the men who were in the houses near Micah’s house gathered together and overtook the children of Dan.
verses.explainer: When they are some distance from Micah's house, the men in the houses near Micah are called out and pursue the Danites. Micah's neighbors rally to help.

verses.text: As they cried to the children of Dan, they turned their faces, and said to Micah, “What ails you, that you come with such a company?”
verses.explainer: They shout at the Danites, who turn and ask Micah: 'What's the matter with you that you called out your men?' The thieves play innocent.

verses.text: He said, “You have taken away my gods which I made, and the priest, and have gone away! What more do I have? How can you ask me, ‘What ails you?’”
verses.explainer: Micah cries: 'You took the gods I made, and my priest, and went away. What else do I have? How can you ask what's the matter?' His anguish reveals how completely he had invested his faith in handmade objects.

thenVsNow.then: Micah's cry -- 'You took the gods I made' -- inadvertently reveals the absurdity of idolatry. Gods you make can be stolen. A god that can be carried away by thieves offers no real security.
thenVsNow.now: Micah's cry -- 'You took the gods I made' -- inadvertently reveals the absurdity of idolatry. Gods you make can be stolen. A god that can be carried away by thieves offers no real security.

verses.text: The children of Dan said to him, “Don’t let your voice be heard among us, lest angry fellows fall on you, and you lose your life, with the lives of your household.”
verses.explainer: The Danites warn: 'Don't argue with us, or some of the men may get angry and attack you, and you and your family will lose your lives.' Might makes right.

verses.text: The children of Dan went their way; and when Micah saw that they were too strong for him, he turned and went back to his house.
verses.explainer: The Danites go on their way, and Micah, seeing they are too strong for him, turns and goes home. He has lost his gods, his priest, and his hope.

verses.text: They took that which Micah had made, and the priest whom he had, and came to Laish, to a people quiet and unsuspecting, and struck them with the edge of the sword; then they burned the city with fire.
verses.explainer: Taking what Micah had made and his priest, the Danites go to Laish, a peaceful and unsuspecting people. They attack them with the sword and burn the city.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The conquest of Laish -- a peaceful, isolated city attacked without provocation -- violates every principle of just war. Dan's aggression was not commanded by God but driven by tribal self-interest and enabled by the victims' vulnerability.

thenVsNow.then: The attack on Laish -- a peaceful, defenseless community -- by an armed Israelite tribe inverts everything Israel should represent. God's people, called to be a blessing to the nations, instead become predators against the vulnerable.
thenVsNow.now: The attack on Laish -- a peaceful, defenseless community -- by an armed Israelite tribe inverts everything Israel should represent. God's people, called to be a blessing to the nations, instead become predators against the vulnerable.

verses.text: There was no deliverer, because it was far from Sidon, and they had no dealings with anyone else; and it was in the valley that lies by Beth Rehob. They built the city, and lived in it.
verses.explainer: No one rescues Laish because it is far from Sidon and has no allies. It is in a valley near Beth-rehob. The isolation that made Laish peaceful also makes it defenseless.

verses.text: They called the name of the city Dan, after the name of Dan their father, who was born to Israel; however the name of the city used to be Laish.
verses.explainer: They rebuild the city and settle there, naming it Dan after their ancestor. The city was previously called Laish. An act of cultural erasure and renaming.

crossReferences.text: Joshua records that Dan's territory was too small and that the Danites attacked Leshem (Laish) and settled there.

verses.text: The children of Dan set up for themselves the engraved image; and Jonathan, the son of Gershom, the son of Moses, and his sons were priests to the tribe of the Danites until the day of the captivity of the land.
verses.explainer: The Danites set up the idol for themselves. Jonathan son of Gershom, son of Moses, and his sons serve as priests for the tribe of Dan until the time of the captivity. The priest is identified as a descendant of Moses -- making the idolatry even more shocking.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The rival shrine at Dan would persist for centuries, eventually becoming one of Jeroboam's two golden calf sites (1 Kings 12:29). What began as one family's idolatry became a national institution.

crossReferences.text: Jeroboam placed one of his two golden calves at Dan, building on the idolatrous tradition established in the judges period.

verses.text: So they set up for themselves Micah’s engraved image which he made, and it remained all the time that God’s house was in Shiloh.
verses.explainer: They continue to use Micah's idol all the time the house of God is in Shiloh. The unauthorized shrine at Dan operates simultaneously with the legitimate tabernacle at Shiloh.

explainer.summary: Chapter 19 tells the most horrifying story in Judges: a Levite's concubine is gang-raped and killed by Israelites in the Benjaminite city of Gibeah. The parallels with Sodom (Genesis 19) are deliberate -- Israel has become no better than the cities God destroyed. The Levite's dismemberment of her body summons the nation to respond.
themes: The complete moral collapse of Israelite society
The failure of hospitality as a sign of social decay
The horrifying treatment of women as disposable
The parallel with Sodom -- Israel becoming what it was supposed to replace
explainer.contextInBook: This chapter is the darkest point in the entire book, demonstrating the full consequences of 'everyone doing what is right in their own eyes.' The story deliberately echoes Sodom to show that Israel has become indistinguishable from the cities God judged.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A secondary wife with lower legal status than a fu
wordStudies.meaning: A secondary wife with lower legal status than a full wife. Concubines had fewer rights and their children had limited inheritance claims. The concubine's vulnerability reflects the precarious status of women without full legal protection.
wordStudies.significance: A secondary wife with lower legal status than a full wife. Concubines had fewer rights and their chi

wordStudies.translatedAs: An act of moral folly so severe it threatens the s
wordStudies.meaning: An act of moral folly so severe it threatens the social fabric. The word implies not just sin but a violation so fundamental it endangers the entire community. It is used for acts that are both wicked and stupid -- destructive of the social order itself.
wordStudies.significance: An act of moral folly so severe it threatens the social fabric. The word implies not just sin but a

verses.text: In those days, when there was no king in Israel, there was a certain Levite living on the farther side of the hill country of Ephraim, who took for himself a concubine out of Bethlehem Judah.
verses.explainer: In those days, when there is no king in Israel, a Levite living in the remote hill country of Ephraim takes a concubine from Bethlehem in Judah. The story begins with the familiar refrain of moral chaos.

verses.text: His concubine played the prostitute against him, and went away from him to her father’s house to Bethlehem Judah, and was there for four months.
verses.explainer: His concubine is unfaithful to him and leaves, returning to her father's house in Bethlehem. She stays there four months. The domestic rupture sets the tragedy in motion.

verses.text: Her husband arose, and went after her, to speak kindly to her, to bring her again, having his servant with him, and a couple of donkeys. She brought him into her father’s house; and when the father of the young lady saw him, he rejoiced to meet him.
verses.explainer: Her husband goes after her to persuade her to return. He takes a servant and two donkeys. When he reaches her father's house, the father welcomes him warmly.

verses.text: His father-in-law, the young lady’s father, kept him there; and he stayed with him three days. So they ate and drank, and stayed there.
verses.explainer: The father-in-law persuades him to stay. He remains three days, eating, drinking, and lodging there. The extended hospitality delays the journey fatefully.

verses.text: On the fourth day, they got up early in the morning, and he rose up to depart. The young lady’s father said to his son-in-law, “Strengthen your heart with a morsel of bread, and afterward you shall go your way.”
verses.explainer: On the fourth day, they get up early to leave, but the father-in-law says: 'Refresh yourself with something to eat; then you can go.' The persuasion to stay continues.

verses.text: So they sat down, ate, and drank, both of them together. Then the young lady’s father said to the man, “Please be pleased to stay all night, and let your heart be merry.”
verses.explainer: They sit down to eat and drink together, and the father-in-law says: 'Please stay tonight and enjoy yourself.' The constant delays will force a late departure with disastrous consequences.

verses.text: The man rose up to depart; but his father-in-law urged him, and he stayed there again.
verses.explainer: The man gets up to leave, but his father-in-law persuades him to stay another night. The hospitality that should be a blessing becomes the instrument of tragedy through its excess.

verses.text: He arose early in the morning on the fifth day to depart; and the young lady’s father said, “Please strengthen your heart and stay until the day declines”; and they both ate.
verses.explainer: On the fifth morning, the father-in-law again urges delay. They eat together until the afternoon. The pattern of delay continues, compressing the available travel time.

verses.text: When the man rose up to depart, he, and his concubine, and his servant, his father-in-law, the young lady’s father, said to him, “Behold, now the day draws toward evening, please stay all night. Behold, the day is ending. Stay here, that your heart may be merry; and tomorrow go on your way early, that you may go home.”
verses.explainer: When the man finally prepares to leave with his concubine and servant, the father-in-law says: 'Look, it's almost evening. Spend the night here.' But this time the man refuses to stay.

verses.text: But the man wouldn’t stay that night, but he rose up and departed, and toward Jebus (also called Jerusalem). With him were a couple of saddled donkeys. His concubine also was with him.
verses.explainer: The man sets out toward Jebus (Jerusalem). He has two saddled donkeys and his concubine. They pass Jebus because it is a Canaanite city and the Levite does not want to stay among foreigners.

verses.text: When they were by Jebus, the day was far spent; and the servant said to his master, “Please come and let us enter into this city of the Jebusites, and stay in it.”
verses.explainer: Near Jebus, the servant suggests spending the night there, but the Levite refuses: 'We won't go into any city whose people are not Israelites. We will go on to Gibeah.' He trusts Israelites over Canaanites.

thenVsNow.then: The Levite's refusal to stay in Jebus because they were 'foreigners' while trusting Israelite Gibeah proves devastatingly ironic. Ethnic or religious identity does not guarantee moral behavior. The foreign city might have been safer.
thenVsNow.now: The Levite's refusal to stay in Jebus because they were 'foreigners' while trusting Israelite Gibeah proves devastatingly ironic. Ethnic or religious identity does not guarantee moral behavior. The foreign city might have been safer.

verses.text: His master said to him, “We won’t enter into the city of a foreigner that is not of the children of Israel; but we will pass over to Gibeah.”
verses.explainer: So they travel on toward Gibeah in Benjamin. They arrive as the sun is setting -- too late for safe travel but hoping for hospitality in an Israelite city.

verses.text: He said to his servant, “Come and let us draw near to one of these places; and we will lodge in Gibeah, or in Ramah.”
verses.explainer: They head for Gibeah or Ramah to spend the night. The sun sets as they approach Gibeah of Benjamin.

verses.text: So they passed on and went their way; and the sun went down on them near Gibeah, which belongs to Benjamin.
verses.explainer: They turn aside to spend the night at Gibeah. The Levite sits in the city square, but no one takes them home for the night. The failure of hospitality is ominous.

verses.text: They went over there, to go in to stay in Gibeah. He went in, and sat down in the street of the city; for there was no one who took them into his house to stay.
verses.explainer: An old man comes in from his work in the fields that evening. He is from the hill country of Ephraim and is living in Gibeah -- a resident alien in a Benjaminite city.

verses.text: Behold, an old man came from his work out of the field at evening. Now the man was from the hill country of Ephraim, and he lived in Gibeah; but the men of the place were Benjamites.
verses.explainer: The old man asks where they are from and where they are going. The Levite explains they are traveling from Bethlehem to the remote hill country of Ephraim.

verses.text: He lifted up his eyes, and saw the wayfaring man in the street of the city; and the old man said, “Where are you going? Where did you come from?”
verses.explainer: The Levite adds: 'No one has taken us into their house. We have straw and fodder for our donkeys and bread and wine for ourselves. We need nothing but a roof.' Their needs are minimal.

verses.text: He said to him, “We are passing from Bethlehem Judah to the farther side of the hill country of Ephraim. I am from there, and I went to Bethlehem Judah. I am going to Yahweh’s house; and there is no one who has taken me into his house.
verses.explainer: The old man insists: 'You are welcome at my house. Let me supply whatever you need. Only don't spend the night in the square.' His urgency suggests he knows the danger of Gibeah's streets at night.

verses.text: Yet there is both straw and feed for our donkeys; and there is bread and wine also for me, and for your servant, and for the young man who is with your servants. There is no lack of anything.”
verses.explainer: The old man takes them to his house and feeds the donkeys. They wash their feet and eat and drink. Proper hospitality is provided by the only righteous person in the city -- a non-native.

verses.text: The old man said, “Peace be to you; how ever let me supply all your needs. Just don’t sleep in the street.”
verses.explainer: While they are enjoying themselves, the men of the city -- wicked men -- surround the house, pounding on the door. They demand: 'Bring out the man who came to your house so we can have sex with him.'

verses.text: So he brought him into his house, and gave the donkeys fodder. Then they washed their feet, and ate and drank.
verses.explainer: The old man goes outside and pleads: 'No, my friends, don't be so vile. Since this man is my guest, don't do this outrageous thing.' He appeals to the sacred obligation of hospitality.

verses.text: As they were making their hearts merry, behold, the men of the city, certain wicked fellows, surrounded the house, beating at the door; and they spoke to the master of the house, the old man, saying, “Bring out the man who came into your house, that we can have sex with him!”
verses.explainer: The owner of the house offers his virgin daughter and the Levite's concubine instead: 'Abuse them and do to them what you wish, but don't do such a vile thing to this man.' The moral calculus is horrifying.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The parallels with Genesis 19 (Sodom) are unmistakable: travelers arrive in a city, one righteous host offers hospitality, a mob demands sexual access to the male guest, the host offers women instead. Israel has become Sodom.

crossReferences.text: The parallel with Sodom is deliberate: the mob at the door, the offer of women instead, the demand for the male guest. Israel has become what it was supposed to replace.

verses.text: The man, the master of the house, went out to them, and said to them, “No, my brothers, please don’t act so wickedly; since this man has come into my house, don’t do this folly.
verses.explainer: But the men will not listen. So the Levite takes his concubine and sends her outside to them. The passive construction shifts to active: he pushes her out.

verses.text: Behold, here is my virgin daughter and his concubine. I will bring them out now. Humble them, and do with them what seems good to you; but to this man don’t do any such folly.”
verses.explainer: They rape her and abuse her throughout the night. At dawn they let her go. She falls at the door of the house where her master is staying, with her hands on the threshold.

verses.text: But the men wouldn’t listen to him: so the man laid hold of his concubine, and brought her out to them; and they had sex with her, and abused her all night until the morning. When the day began to dawn, they let her go.
verses.explainer: In the morning, the Levite opens the door to go on his way. There lies his concubine, fallen at the doorway with her hands on the threshold. The image is devastating in its simplicity.

verses.text: Then the woman came in the dawning of the day, and fell down at the door of the man’s house where her lord was, until it was light.
verses.explainer: He says to her: 'Get up. Let's go.' But there is no answer. So he puts her on his donkey and sets out for home. His callousness is chilling -- no grief, no rage, just 'get up, let's go.'

verses.text: Her lord rose up in the morning, and opened the doors of the house, and went out to go his way; and behold, the woman his concubine had fallen down at the door of the house, with her hands on the threshold.
verses.explainer: When he reaches home, he takes a knife and cuts her body into twelve pieces, limb by limb, and sends them throughout all the territory of Israel. The dismemberment is a gruesome summons to action.

verses.text: He said to her, “Get up, and let us be going!” but no one answered. Then he took her up on the donkey; and the man rose up, and went to his place.
verses.explainer: Everyone who sees the pieces says: 'Such a thing has never been seen or done since the day Israel came up out of Egypt. Just imagine! Think about it! Tell us what to do!' The horror provokes a national response.

thenVsNow.then: The Levite's command -- 'Get up. Let's go' -- to his dying or dead concubine reveals a moral vacancy as horrifying as the crime itself. The victim is treated as a possession, not a person, even by the one who should protect her.
thenVsNow.now: The Levite's command -- 'Get up. Let's go' -- to his dying or dead concubine reveals a moral vacancy as horrifying as the crime itself. The victim is treated as a possession, not a person, even by the one who should protect her.

verses.text: When he had come into his house, he took a knife, and cut up his concubine, and divided her, limb by limb, into twelve pieces, and sent her throughout all the borders of Israel.
verses.explainer: The concubine's body parts serve as a call to assembly -- a shocking, visceral demand that Israel cannot ignore.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Sending body parts throughout Israel as a summons to war was an extreme but known practice. Saul would later do the same with oxen (1 Samuel 11:7) to rally Israel against the Ammonites.

crossReferences.text: Saul later cut oxen into pieces and sent them throughout Israel to summon troops, using the same shock tactic.

verses.text: It was so, that all who saw it said, “Such a deed has not been done or seen from the day that the children of Israel came up out of the land of Egypt to this day! Consider it, take counsel, and speak.”
verses.explainer: All who see it declare: 'Nothing like this has happened since Israel came out of Egypt. Consider it! Take counsel! Speak!' The nation is summoned to respond to the moral catastrophe.

explainer.summary: Chapter 20 describes the civil war against Benjamin. After two devastating defeats, Israel finally destroys the Benjaminite army using the same ambush strategy that worked at Ai. The near-annihilation of Benjamin -- leaving only 600 survivors -- demonstrates how quickly a society without moral authority descends into self-destruction.
themes: The tragedy of civil war within God's people
Divine sovereignty in allowing initial defeats
The devastating consequences of protecting evil
The near-extinction of a tribe as the cost of moral failure
explainer.contextInBook: The civil war is the climax of the book's downward spiral. Israel, which could not unite against foreign enemies, now unites to destroy a brother tribe. The same military strategies used against Canaan are turned against Benjamin.

wordStudies.translatedAs: United as a single individual
wordStudies.meaning: United as a single individual. This phrase, describing Israel's unity against Benjamin, is deeply ironic: the unity never achieved against foreign enemies is perfectly achieved for civil war.
wordStudies.significance: United as a single individual. This phrase, describing Israel's unity against Benjamin, is deeply ir

wordStudies.translatedAs: A Deuteronomic phrase for removing moral pollution
wordStudies.meaning: A Deuteronomic phrase for removing moral pollution from the community. The same command applied to individual criminals is now applied to an entire tribe, showing how corporate complicity spreads individual guilt.
wordStudies.significance: A Deuteronomic phrase for removing moral pollution from the community. The same command applied to i

verses.text: Then all the children of Israel went out, and the congregation was assembled as one man, from Dan even to Beersheba, with the land of Gilead, to Yahweh at Mizpah.
verses.explainer: All Israel from Dan to Beersheba, including Gilead, assembles before the LORD at Mizpah. The response is total -- the full national assembly gathers.

verses.text: The chiefs of all the people, even of all the tribes of Israel, presented themselves in the assembly of the people of God, four hundred thousand footmen who drew sword.
verses.explainer: The leaders of all the people and all the tribes present themselves -- four hundred thousand men armed with swords. The largest army assembled in Judges.

verses.text: (Now the children of Benjamin heard that the children of Israel had gone up to Mizpah.) The children of Israel said, “Tell us, how did this wickedness happen?”
verses.explainer: The Benjaminites hear that the Israelites have gone up to Mizpah. The Levite is asked to explain what happened.

verses.text: The Levite, the husband of the woman who was murdered, answered, “I came into Gibeah that belongs to Benjamin, I and my concubine, to spend the night.
verses.explainer: The Levite testifies: 'I and my concubine came to Gibeah in Benjamin to spend the night.' His account emphasizes the Benjaminites' guilt.

verses.text: The men of Gibeah rose against me, and surrounded the house by night. They intended to kill me, and they raped my concubine, and she is dead.
verses.explainer: He continues: 'During the night the men of Gibeah came after me and surrounded the house, intending to kill me. They raped my concubine, and she died.' He minimizes his own role.

verses.text: I took my concubine, and cut her in pieces, and sent her throughout all the country of the inheritance of Israel; for they have committed lewdness and folly in Israel.
verses.explainer: He explains: 'I took my concubine, cut her into pieces, and sent them into all the areas of Israel's inheritance, because they committed this lewd and outrageous act in Israel.' His summary omits his own complicity.

verses.text: Behold, you children of Israel, all of you, give here your advice and counsel.”
verses.explainer: The Levite demands: 'Now, all you Israelites, speak up and tell me what you have decided to do.' He appeals for collective action.

verses.text: All the people arose as one man, saying, “None of us will go to his tent, neither will any of us turn to his house.
verses.explainer: All the people rise as one: 'None of us will go home. None of us will return to our houses.' The response is unanimous and determined.

verses.text: But now this is the thing which we will do to Gibeah: we will go up against it by lot;
verses.explainer: They decide by lot who will attack Gibeah. The army organizes for a campaign against a fellow Israelite tribe.

verses.text: and we will take ten men of one hundred throughout all the tribes of Israel, and one hundred of one thousand, and a thousand out of ten thousand, to get food for the people, that they may do, when they come to Gibeah of Benjamin, according to all the folly that they have worked in Israel.”
verses.explainer: They select one-tenth of the army for supply duty: ten thousand out of every hundred thousand, to provide food for the army. Logistics precede battle.

verses.text: So all the men of Israel were gathered against the city, knit together as one man.
verses.explainer: All Israel unites against the city, united as one. The Hebrew emphasizes total solidarity -- the same unity never achieved against foreign enemies is now directed against a brother tribe.

verses.text: The tribes of Israel sent men through all the tribe of Benjamin, saying, “What wickedness is this that has happened among you?
verses.explainer: The tribes send messengers throughout Benjamin: 'What about this awful crime committed among you? Hand over those wicked men of Gibeah so we can put them to death and purge the evil from Israel.' They give Benjamin a chance to resolve it peacefully.

verses.text:
Now therefore deliver up the men, the wicked fellows, who are in Gibeah, that we may put them to death, and put away evil from Israel.”
But Benjamin would not listen to the voice of their brothers the children of Israel.
verses.explainer: But the Benjaminites refuse to listen to their fellow Israelites. Instead, they mobilize from their towns to fight Israel at Gibeah. Benjamin chooses tribal solidarity over justice.

thenVsNow.then: Benjamin's choice to protect the guilty rather than surrender them to justice resulted in the near-extinction of the entire tribe. Corporate protection of wrongdoers spreads the guilt -- and the consequences -- to the entire community.
thenVsNow.now: Benjamin's choice to protect the guilty rather than surrender them to justice resulted in the near-extinction of the entire tribe. Corporate protection of wrongdoers spreads the guilt -- and the consequences -- to the entire community.

verses.text: The children of Benjamin gathered themselves together out of the cities to Gibeah, to go out to battle against the children of Israel.
verses.explainer: Benjamin musters twenty-six thousand swordsmen from their towns, plus seven hundred select men from Gibeah itself. A small but formidable force.

verses.text: The children of Benjamin were counted on that day out of the cities twenty-six thousand men who drew the sword, besides the inhabitants of Gibeah, who were counted seven hundred chosen men.
verses.explainer: Among all these soldiers are seven hundred select left-handed men, each of whom can sling a stone at a hair and not miss. Elite warriors from the tribe of the 'right hand' who fight left-handed.

verses.text: Among all these soldiers there were seven hundred chosen men who were left-handed. Every one of them could sling a stone at a hair and not miss.
verses.explainer: Israel, apart from Benjamin, musters four hundred thousand swordsmen, all of them fighting men. The numerical advantage is overwhelming, yet the first battles will be disastrous.

verses.text: The men of Israel, besides Benjamin, were counted four hundred thousand men who drew sword. All these were men of war.
verses.explainer: Israel assembles and musters its forces for the campaign against its own brother tribe.

verses.text:
The children of Israel arose, went up to Bethel, and asked counsel of God. They asked, “Who shall go up for us first to battle against the children of Benjamin?”
Yahweh said, “Judah first.”
verses.explainer: The Israelites go up to Bethel and inquire of God: 'Who shall go first to fight against the Benjaminites?' The LORD answers: 'Judah shall go first.' The same answer given in chapter 1.

verses.text: The children of Israel rose up in the morning, and encamped against Gibeah.
verses.explainer: The Israelites rise the next morning and encamp against Gibeah. The campaign begins.

verses.text: The men of Israel went out to battle against Benjamin; and the men of Israel set the battle in array against them at Gibeah.
verses.explainer: Israel goes out to fight Benjamin and takes up battle positions against Gibeah. The first attack is launched.

verses.text: The children of Benjamin came out of Gibeah, and on that day destroyed twenty-two thousand of the Israelite men down to the ground.
verses.explainer: The Benjaminites come out from Gibeah and cut down twenty-two thousand Israelites on that day. A shocking defeat for the much larger army.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Two initial defeats of the much larger army teach that numerical superiority does not guarantee victory. God allowed the defeats to deepen Israel's dependence on Him and to purge any arrogance from their righteous cause.

crossReferences.text: Jacob's prophecy of Benjamin: 'Benjamin is a ravenous wolf.' The tribe's ferocity in battle fulfills this ancient characterization.

thenVsNow.then: Israel's two initial defeats despite being on the side of justice remind us that doing the right thing does not guarantee an easy path. God may allow setbacks to test our commitment to justice.
thenVsNow.now: Israel's two initial defeats despite being on the side of justice remind us that doing the right thing does not guarantee an easy path. God may allow setbacks to test our commitment to justice.

verses.text: The people, the men of Israel, encouraged themselves, and set the battle again in array in the place where they set themselves in array the first day.
verses.explainer: But the Israelites encourage one another and again take up positions where they had stationed themselves the first day. They refuse to accept the defeat as final.

verses.text:
The children of Israel went up and wept before Yahweh until evening; and they asked of Yahweh, saying, “Shall I again draw near to battle against the children of Benjamin my brother?”
Yahweh said, “Go up against him.”
verses.explainer: The Israelites go up and weep before the LORD until evening. They inquire: 'Shall we go up again to fight our brothers the Benjaminites?' The LORD says: 'Go up against them.' God sanctions the war but does not guarantee easy victory.

verses.text: The children of Israel came near against the children of Benjamin the second day.
verses.explainer: On the second day, Israel approaches Benjamin again. The Israelites advance against Gibeah a second time.

verses.text: Benjamin went out against them out of Gibeah the second day, and destroyed down to the ground of the children of Israel again eighteen thousand men. All these drew the sword.
verses.explainer: Benjamin comes out from Gibeah against them and cuts down another eighteen thousand Israelites, all armed with swords. The second defeat is nearly as devastating as the first.

verses.text: Then all the children of Israel, and all the people, went up, and came to Bethel, and wept, and sat there before Yahweh, and fasted that day until evening; then they offered burnt offerings and peace offerings before Yahweh.
verses.explainer: All the Israelites go to Bethel, sit before the LORD, weep, fast until evening, and offer burnt offerings and fellowship offerings. This time they add fasting and sacrifice to their inquiry.

verses.text: The children of Israel asked Yahweh (for the ark of the covenant of God was there in those days,
verses.explainer: The Israelites inquire of the LORD. (The ark of the covenant of God is there in those days, with Phinehas son of Eleazar, son of Aaron, ministering before it.) This genealogical note places the story in the early judges period.

verses.text:
and Phinehas, the son of Eleazar, the son of Aaron, stood before it in those days), saying, “Shall I yet again go out to battle against the children of Benjamin my brother, or shall I cease?”
Yahweh said, “Go up; for tomorrow I will deliver him into your hand.”
verses.explainer: They ask: 'Shall we go up again to fight the Benjaminites, or shall we stop?' The LORD answers: 'Go up; tomorrow I will give them into your hands.' This time God promises victory.

verses.text: Israel set ambushes all around Gibeah.
verses.explainer: Israel sets an ambush around Gibeah. The strategy shifts from frontal assault to tactical deception.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The ambush strategy mirrors Joshua's defeat of Ai (Joshua 8). The same tactics used against Canaan are now used against fellow Israelites -- a grim commentary on how far things have fallen.

crossReferences.text: The ambush strategy against Gibeah directly parallels Joshua's strategy against Ai: feigned retreat, ambush from behind, smoke signal, and complete destruction.

verses.text: The children of Israel went up against the children of Benjamin on the third day, and set themselves in array against Gibeah, as at other times.
verses.explainer: On the third day, Israel goes up against Benjamin and takes positions, as before. The initial advance looks identical to the previous two attacks.

verses.text: The children of Benjamin went out against the people, and were drawn away from the city; and they began to strike and kill of the people, as at other times, in the highways, of which one goes up to Bethel, and the other to Gibeah, in the field, about thirty men of Israel.
verses.explainer: Benjamin comes out and is drawn away from the city. They begin to inflict casualties on Israel along the roads -- about thirty men. Benjamin thinks a third rout is underway.

verses.text: The children of Benjamin said, “They are struck down before us, as at the first.” But the children of Israel said, “Let us flee, and draw them away from the city to the highways.”
verses.explainer: The Benjaminites say: 'We are defeating them as before.' But the Israelites are deliberately retreating to draw them away from the city. The ruse is working.

verses.text: All the men of Israel rose up out of their place, and set themselves in array at Baal Tamar. Then the ambushers of Israel broke out of their place, even out of Maareh Geba.
verses.explainer: All the Israelites move from their positions and form up at Baal-tamar while the ambush charges out from its position west of Gibeah. The trap springs.

verses.text: Ten thousand chosen men out of all Israel came over against Gibeah, and the battle was severe; but they didn’t know that disaster was close to them.
verses.explainer: Ten thousand select Israelite soldiers attack Gibeah head-on. The fighting is fierce, and the Benjaminites do not realize disaster is about to overtake them.

verses.text: Yahweh struck Benjamin before Israel; and the children of Israel destroyed of Benjamin that day twenty-five thousand one hundred men. All these drew the sword.
verses.explainer: The LORD defeats Benjamin before Israel. That day Israel destroys twenty-five thousand one hundred Benjaminite swordsmen. The victory is attributed to God.

verses.text: So the children of Benjamin saw that they were struck; for the men of Israel yielded to Benjamin, because they trusted the ambushers whom they had set against Gibeah.
verses.explainer: The Benjaminites see they are defeated. The Israelite ruse -- feigned retreat followed by ambush -- mirrors the strategy used against Ai in Joshua 8.

verses.text: The ambushers hurried, and rushed on Gibeah; then the ambushers spread out, and struck all the city with the edge of the sword.
verses.explainer: The ambush force rushes into Gibeah, spreads out, and puts the whole city to the sword. The hidden force attacks the now-undefended city.

verses.text: Now the appointed sign between the men of Israel and the ambushers was that they should make a great cloud of smoke rise up out of the city.
verses.explainer: The Israelites had arranged a signal with the ambush: when a great cloud of smoke rises from the city, the main force should turn and attack. The coordination is precise.

verses.text: The men of Israel turned in the battle, and Benjamin began to strike and kill of the men of Israel about thirty persons; for they said, “Surely they are struck down before us, as in the first battle.”
verses.explainer: When the Benjaminites have killed about thirty Israelites and think they are winning as before, the column of smoke begins to rise from the city. Benjamin's confidence turns to horror.

verses.text: But when the cloud began to arise up out of the city in a pillar of smoke, the Benjamites looked behind them; and behold, the whole city went up in smoke to the sky.
verses.explainer: When the Benjaminites turn and see the smoke of the whole city going up, they realize the ambush has taken the city. Confidence becomes panic.

verses.text: The men of Israel turned, and the men of Benjamin were dismayed; for they saw that disaster had come on them.
verses.explainer: The Israelites turn on them, and the Benjaminites are terrified because they see that disaster has come upon them. The trap is complete.

verses.text: Therefore they turned their backs before the men of Israel to the way of the wilderness; but the battle followed hard after them; and those who came out of the cities destroyed them in the middle of it.
verses.explainer: They flee before the Israelites toward the wilderness, but the battle overtakes them. The men coming out of the towns cut them down. There is no escape.

verses.text: They surrounded the Benjamites, chased them, and trod them down at their resting place, as far as near Gibeah toward the sunrise.
verses.explainer: They surround the Benjaminites, chase them, and easily overrun them east of Gibeah. The pursuit is relentless.

verses.text: Eighteen thousand men of Benjamin fell; all these were men of valor.
verses.explainer: Eighteen thousand Benjaminites fall -- all of them valiant fighters. The casualties are enormous for a single tribe.

verses.text: They turned and fled toward the wilderness to the rock of Rimmon; and they gleaned five thousand men of them in the highways, and followed hard after them to Gidom, and struck two thousand men of them.
verses.explainer: The survivors turn and flee toward the wilderness to the rock of Rimmon. Israel catches five thousand on the roads and pursues them to Gidom, striking down two thousand more.

verses.text: So that all who fell that day of Benjamin were twenty-five thousand men who drew the sword. All these were men of valor.
verses.explainer: On that day twenty-five thousand Benjaminite swordsmen fall -- all of them valiant fighters. Benjamin is nearly annihilated.

verses.text: But six hundred men turned and fled toward the wilderness to the rock of Rimmon, and stayed in the rock of Rimmon four months.
verses.explainer: But six hundred men turn and flee to the rock of Rimmon, where they stay four months. The remnant of Benjamin -- six hundred survivors from an entire tribe.

verses.text: The men of Israel turned again on the children of Benjamin, and struck them with the edge of the sword, both the entire city, and the livestock, and all that they found. Moreover all the cities which they found they set on fire.
verses.explainer: The Israelites turn back against Benjamin and put to the sword the entire city -- people, animals, everything they find. They set fire to all the towns they come across. Total war against a brother tribe.

explainer.summary: Chapter 21 shows Israel trying to preserve the tribe of Benjamin after nearly destroying it. Unable to give their own daughters due to a rash oath, they massacre the city of Jabesh Gilead to obtain wives, then organize the kidnapping of women dancing at Shiloh's festival. The book ends with its devastating refrain: 'Everyone did what was right in their own eyes.'
themes: Rash oaths creating new crises
Moral bankruptcy in the name of tribal preservation
Violence against women as an acceptable solution
The final repetition of the book's thesis: moral chaos without moral authority
explainer.contextInBook: The final chapter brings the book to its devastating conclusion. Israel's 'solution' to the Benjamin crisis involves two additional atrocities (the Jabesh Gilead massacre and the Shiloh kidnappings), showing that the moral decay is so deep that even attempts to do right produce more evil.

wordStudies.greek: Everyone did what was right in their own eyes
wordStudies.transliteration: Everyone did what was right in their own eyes
wordStudies.translatedAs: The final statement of the book's thesis
wordStudies.meaning: The final statement of the book's thesis. Moral autonomy without moral authority produces not freedom but chaos. The phrase is both a diagnosis (this is what happened) and an argument (this is why Israel needs a king).
wordStudies.significance: The final statement of the book's thesis. Moral autonomy without moral authority produces not freedo

wordStudies.meaning: An annual religious celebration at Shiloh, likely the Feast of Tabernacles. The holy festival becomes the setting for organized kidnapping -- the ultimate corruption of sacred space and time.
