verses.text: Now the men of Israel had sworn in Mizpah, saying, “None of us will give his daughter to Benjamin as a wife.”
verses.explainer: The men of Israel had sworn at Mizpah: 'Not one of us will give his daughter in marriage to a Benjaminite.' The oath, made in the heat of righteous anger, creates a new crisis.

verses.text: The people came to Bethel, and sat there until evening before God, and lifted up their voices, and wept severely.
verses.explainer: The people go to Bethel, sit before God until evening, raising their voices and weeping bitterly. Having nearly destroyed a brother tribe, they now mourn the result of their own extremism.

verses.text: They said, “Yahweh, the God of Israel, why has this happened in Israel, that there should be one tribe lacking in Israel today?”
verses.explainer: They cry out: 'LORD, God of Israel, why has this happened? Today one tribe is missing from Israel.' Their anguish is genuine but self-inflicted -- they created the problem they now weep over.

verses.text: On the next day, the people rose early, and built an altar there, and offered burnt offerings and peace offerings.
verses.explainer: The next day the people build an altar and offer burnt offerings and fellowship offerings. They seek God's guidance for a problem they created through their own rash oath.

verses.text: The children of Israel said, “Who is there among all the tribes of Israel who didn’t come up in the assembly to Yahweh?” For they had made a great oath concerning him who didn’t come up to Yahweh to Mizpah, saying, “He shall surely be put to death.”
verses.explainer: They ask: 'Which tribe did not come to the assembly at Mizpah?' A loophole emerges: if any community failed to attend the assembly, they are not bound by the oath and can be attacked to provide wives.

verses.text: The children of Israel grieved for Benjamin their brother, and said, “There is one tribe cut off from Israel today.
verses.explainer: The Israelites grieve for their brothers the Benjaminites: 'Today one tribe is cut off from Israel.' The grief is sincere but the solution they devise is horrifying.

verses.text: How shall we provide wives for those who remain, since we have sworn by Yahweh that we will not give them of our daughters to wives?”
verses.explainer: How can we provide wives for the survivors, since we have sworn by the LORD not to give our daughters to them? The oath constrains them but their 'solution' will be worse than the problem.

verses.text: They said, “What one is there of the tribes of Israel who didn’t come up to Yahweh to Mizpah?” Behold, no one came from Jabesh Gilead to the camp to the assembly.
verses.explainer: They discover that no one from Jabesh Gilead came to the assembly. Jabesh Gilead's absence makes them a target.

verses.text: For when the people were counted, behold, there were none of the inhabitants of Jabesh Gilead there.
verses.explainer: They count the assembled people and find no one from Jabesh Gilead. The investigation identifies the absent community as the source of potential wives.

verses.text: The congregation sent twelve thousand of the most valiant men there, and commanded them, saying, “Go and strike the inhabitants of Jabesh Gilead with the edge of the sword, with the women and the little ones.
verses.explainer: The assembly sends twelve thousand fighting men with orders: 'Go and put to the sword those living in Jabesh Gilead, including the women and children.' The punishment for non-attendance is massacre.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The massacre of Jabesh Gilead to obtain wives represents the complete inversion of moral reasoning. A community is destroyed not for any crime it committed but to provide women for another community's needs.

thenVsNow.then: The 'solutions' devised to save Benjamin -- massacre and kidnapping -- demonstrate that communities in moral freefall cannot solve their problems because their problem-solving is itself corrupted by the same moral decay.
thenVsNow.now: The 'solutions' devised to save Benjamin -- massacre and kidnapping -- demonstrate that communities in moral freefall cannot solve their problems because their problem-solving is itself corrupted by the same moral decay.

verses.text: This is the thing that you shall do: you shall utterly destroy every male, and every woman who has lain with a man.”
verses.explainer: The instructions specify: destroy every male and every woman who is not a virgin. They are to kill an entire city to provide wives for the men of another tribe.

verses.text: They found among the inhabitants of Jabesh Gilead four hundred young virgins, who had not known man by lying with him; and they brought them to the camp to Shiloh, which is in the land of Canaan.
verses.explainer: They find among the inhabitants of Jabesh Gilead four hundred young women who have never slept with a man, and bring them to the camp at Shiloh. Four hundred of the needed six hundred wives are obtained through massacre.

crossReferences.text: Saul's first act as king was rescuing Jabesh Gilead from the Ammonites -- perhaps motivated by the kinship connection established here through the Benjamin marriages.

verses.text: The whole congregation sent and spoke to the children of Benjamin who were in the rock of Rimmon, and proclaimed peace to them.
verses.explainer: The assembly sends a peace delegation to the Benjaminites at the rock of Rimmon, offering peace. Having nearly destroyed them, Israel now offers reconciliation.

verses.text: Benjamin returned at that time; and they gave them the women whom they had saved alive of the women of Jabesh Gilead. There still weren’t enough for them.
verses.explainer: Benjamin returns. The four hundred women of Jabesh Gilead are given to them, but there are not enough for all of them. Two hundred men remain without wives.

verses.text: The people grieved for Benjamin, because Yahweh had made a breach in the tribes of Israel.
verses.explainer: The people grieve for Benjamin because the LORD has made a gap in the tribes of Israel. They attribute to God what their own actions caused.

verses.text: Then the elders of the congregation said, “How shall we provide wives for those who remain, since the women are destroyed out of Benjamin?”
verses.explainer: The elders of the assembly ask: 'How shall we provide wives for the remaining men, since the women of Benjamin were destroyed?' The problem-solving continues down an increasingly dark path.

verses.text: They said, “There must be an inheritance for those who are escaped of Benjamin, that a tribe not be blotted out from Israel.
verses.explainer: They reason: 'There must be heirs for the surviving Benjaminites, so that a tribe of Israel will not be wiped out.' Tribal preservation justifies further extremism.

verses.text: However we may not give them wives of our daughters, for the children of Israel had sworn, saying, ‘Cursed is he who gives a wife to Benjamin.’”
verses.explainer: But 'we can't give them our daughters as wives, since we have taken an oath.' The oath remains binding even though its consequences have proven catastrophic.

verses.text: They said, “Behold, there is a feast of Yahweh from year to year in Shiloh, which is on the north of Bethel, on the east side of the highway that goes up from Bethel to Shechem, and on the south of Lebonah.”
verses.explainer: They find another loophole: the annual festival of the LORD at Shiloh. They instruct the Benjaminites to hide in the vineyards.

verses.text: They commanded the children of Benjamin, saying, “Go and lie in wait in the vineyards,
verses.explainer: They tell the Benjaminites: 'Go and hide in the vineyards. When the young women of Shiloh come out to dance, rush from the vineyards and each of you seize a wife.' Organized kidnapping is presented as a solution.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The organized kidnapping at Shiloh's festival echoes the Roman legend of the rape of the Sabine women. Both stories describe communities using violence to obtain wives, reflecting the brutal realities of ancient tribal survival.

verses.text: and see, and behold, if the daughters of Shiloh come out to dance in the dances, then come out of the vineyards, and each man catch his wife of the daughters of Shiloh, and go to the land of Benjamin.
verses.explainer: They plan to intercept any complaints: 'When their fathers or brothers complain, we will say: Do us the kindness of helping them, because we did not get wives for them during the war.'

verses.text: It shall be, when their fathers or their brothers come to complain to us, that we will say to them, ‘Grant them graciously to us, because we didn’t take for each man his wife in battle, neither did you give them to them, otherwise you would now be guilty.’”
verses.explainer: The excuse continues: 'You did not violate your oath because you didn't voluntarily give them your daughters.' The legalistic distinction between 'giving' and 'having taken' is morally bankrupt.

verses.text: The children of Benjamin did so, and took wives for themselves, according to their number, of those who danced, whom they carried off. They went and returned to their inheritance, built the cities, and lived in them.
verses.explainer: The Benjaminites do as instructed. Each man catches a wife from the dancers at Shiloh. They return to their territory, rebuild their towns, and settle down.

verses.text: The children of Israel departed from there at that time, every man to his tribe and to his family, and they each went out from there to his own inheritance.
verses.explainer: The Israelites disperse, each to their own tribe and clan, each to their own inheritance. The crisis ends with everyone going home.

verses.text: In those days there was no king in Israel. Everyone did that which was right in his own eyes.
verses.explainer: In those days Israel had no king; everyone did as they saw fit. The book's final word: the refrain that explains everything and excuses nothing.

crossReferences.text: The implicit argument of Judges' refrain -- that Israel needs a king -- is fulfilled when Israel demands a king from Samuel.

thenVsNow.then: The book ends not with resolution but with a diagnosis. 'Everyone did what was right in their own eyes' is not a celebration of freedom but a lament over chaos. The story demands a response: what kind of authority can restore moral order?
